# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEMUT722, Module 2]**
**Q: Define Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigeration system used in automotive air-conditioning. Why is COP preferred over efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** COP of a refrigeration system is the ratio of the desired cooling effect $Q_L$ to the work input $W_{comp}$ required. Mathematically, $COP = Q_L / W_{comp}$. For an ideal cycle, $COP = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ where temperatures are in Kelvin.
- **Why preferred (1.5 marks):** COP is dimensionless and directly expresses the **useful cooling per unit of energy spent**. A COP of 3 means 3 units of cooling for 1 unit of electrical/mechanical work. Unlike "efficiency", COP can exceed 1, which is normal for heat pumps and refrigerators, reflecting that they **move** heat rather than create it.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEMUT722, Module 2]**
**Q: What is Sound Transmission Loss (STL)? State the Mass Law and its significance in cabin noise control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **STL definition (1 mark):** STL is the ratio in dB of acoustic power incident on a partition to that transmitted through it, $STL = 10 \log_{10}(W_i / W_t)$.
- **Mass Law (1.5 marks):** For a single panel, $STL = 20 \log_{10}(mf) - 47$, where $m$ is the surface density (kg/m²) and $f$ is frequency (Hz). It states that **doubling the mass or frequency increases STL by 6 dB**.
- **Significance (0.5 marks):** It guides the design of firewalls, doors, and dash insulators to achieve the OEM target cabin SPL (typically ≤ 65 dB(A)).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEMUT722, Module 2]**

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an automotive HVAC system. Discuss the role of the blend door, mode door, and recirculation flap.

**(b) [7 marks]** A car AC operates on the vapour-compression cycle with R-134a. The compressor inlet state is at 0.2 MPa with enthalpy 247 kJ/kg and entropy 0.93 kJ/kg·K. The compressor outlet pressure is 1.2 MPa. Saturated liquid leaves the condenser at 111 kJ/kg. The mass flow rate of refrigerant is 0.08 kg/s. Calculate (i) compressor work, (ii) refrigeration effect, (iii) cooling capacity in kW, and (iv) COP. Isentropic efficiency of compressor = 0.7.

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** State the function of HVAC — maintain cabin thermal comfort by controlling temperature, humidity, air purity, and air distribution.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks):** Draw and label the block diagram. Reference Section 4.1 of these notes. Key blocks: Intake → Filter → Blower → Evaporator → Heater Core → Blend Door → Plenum → Mode Doors → Face/Feet/Defrost Vents.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Explain the three actuators:
- **Blend Door (Air-Mix Door):** Proportionally mixes cold air (post-evaporator) with hot air (post-heater core) to deliver air at the user-set temperature. PID-controlled.
- **Mode Door:** Directs conditioned air to face, feet, bi-level, or defrost outlets. Critical for demist logic in winter.
- **Recirculation Flap:** Switches between fresh-air mode and recirculation mode. Recirculation cools faster and isolates cabin from tunnel smoke/pollution; fresh-air mode prevents window fogging.

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compressor outlet enthalpy (isentropic):** From R-134a superheated tables at 1.2 MPa and $s = 0.93$ kJ/kg·K: $h_{2s} = 273$ kJ/kg.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Actual compressor outlet enthalpy (using isentropic efficiency):**

$$
h_2 = h_1 + \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{\eta_{is}} = 247 + \frac{273 - 247}{0.7} = 247 + 37.14 = 284.14 \, kJ/kg
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Compressor work per kg:**

$$
w_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 284.14 - 247 = 37.14 \, kJ/kg
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Refrigeration effect per kg (throttle is isenthalpic so $h_4 = h_3$):**

$$
q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 247 - 111 = 136 \, kJ/kg
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Cooling capacity:**

$$
\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \cdot q_L = 0.08 \times 136 = 10.88 \, kW
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Compressor power:**

$$
\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{comp} = 0.08 \times 37.14 = 2.97 \, kW
$$

**Step 7 (0.5 marks) — Coefficient of Performance:**

$$
COP = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}} = \frac{10.88}{2.97} \approx 3.66
$$

> This is realistic for a vehicle AC system, confirming the design is sound.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, OEMUT722, Module 2]**

**Q: (a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of Noise, Vibration and Harshness (NVH) in automobiles. List the major sources of cabin noise and the engineering strategies used to attenuate them.

**(b) [7 marks]** A car door panel has a surface density of 6 kg/m². The engine produces dominant noise at 250 Hz. Calculate the Sound Transmission Loss (STL) of the door panel using the Mass Law. If a second identical panel is added with an air gap of 50 mm (treating the double panel as $m_{eff} = 6$ kg/m² but with a 9 dB coincidence improvement at 250 Hz), find the new effective STL. Comment on whether the cabin SPL will meet the OEM target of ≤ 65 dB(A) if the engine SPL at the panel is 95 dB(A).

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define NVH — the interdisciplinary engineering domain that studies noise and vibration in vehicles to refine ride quality, structural durability, and acoustic comfort.

**Step 2 (2.5 marks):** Classify noise sources:
- **Powertrain NVH:** engine combustion, valve-train, exhaust, transmission whine (20 Hz – 4 kHz).
- **Road NVH:** tire-pavement interaction, suspension impacts (100 Hz – 5 kHz).
- **Aerodynamic NVH:** wind turbulence, A-pillar whistle, side-mirror vortex (500 Hz – 10 kHz).
- **Structure-borne:** engine mounts, exhaust hangers, driveline.

**Step 3 (3.5 marks):** Attenuation strategies:
- **Source control:** engine encapsulation, balance shafts, helical gears, exhaust mufflers.
- **Path control — Absorption:** porous foams (PU, melamine) in dash, roof, wheel-arch liners.
- **Path control — Damping:** constrained-layer damping (CLD) sheets, bitumen pads on body panels.
- **Path control — Isolation:** engine mounts (hydraulic), sub-frame bushings.
- **Receiver control:** laminated glass, ANC headphones, soundproof cabin trim.
- **Sealing:** weather-strip door seals, airtight HVAC gaskets.

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — STL of single panel at 250 Hz using Mass Law:**

$$
STL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(m \cdot f) - 47 = 20 \log_{10}(6 \times 250) - 47
$$

$$
STL_1 = 20 \log_{10}(1500) - 47 = 20 \times 3.176 - 47 = 63.52 - 47 = 16.52 \, dB
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Effective STL of double panel (with 9 dB improvement):**

$$
STL_2 = STL_1 + 9 = 16.52 + 9 = 25.52 \, dB
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Transmitted SPL to cabin:**

$$
SPL_{cabin} = SPL_{source} - STL = 95 - 25.52 = 69.48 \, dB(A)
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Comparison to OEM target:**

> Since $SPL_{cabin} = 69.48 \, dB(A) > 65 \, dB(A)$ OEM target, the design **fails** to meet the specification.

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Required additional attenuation:**

$$
\Delta STL_{required} = 69.48 - 65 = 4.48 \, dB
$$

> **Recommendation:** Add **3 mm constrained-layer damping sheet** (typical $\eta = 0.3$, providing ~6 dB at 250 Hz) on the door panel, or increase the panel mass to $m_{new}$ where:
>
> $$20 \log_{10}(m_{new} \cdot 250) - 47 \geq 25.52 + 4.48 = 30 \, dB$$
> $$m_{new} \cdot 250 \geq 10^{(30+47)/20} = 10^{3.85} = 7079$$
> $$m_{new} \geq 28.3 \, kg/m^2$$
>
> This shows that **damping is far more mass-efficient** than adding steel — a key KTU board insight.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **SPL Addition Error (–2 marks):** Adding dB values arithmetically. Always use $L_{tot} = 10 \log \Sigma 10^{L_i/10}$. The "3 dB rule" only works for two equal sources.
> 2. **COP Confusion (–2 marks):** Writing $COP = W/Q_L$ instead of $Q_L/W$. Memorize: COP is **output over input**.
> 3. **Throttle Process (–1 mark):** Forgetting that throttling is **isenthalpic** ($h_3 = h_4$), not isentropic.
> 4. **Mass Law Constants (–1 mark):** Using −47 in SI units is correct; using −42 or −36.4 without justifying is penalized.
> 5. **Demist Logic (–2 marks):** Writing that "AC is switched off in winter" — wrong. AC is engaged in winter for **dehumidification** to clear fogged glass.
> 6. **A-Weighting vs Linear SPL (–1 mark):** Forgetting to add "(A)" when the question asks for human-perceived noise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- ✅ HVAC = **Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning**; four controlled parameters: **T, RH, v, purity**.
- ✅ Refrigeration cycle = **Vapour Compression**; four processes: compression, condensation, throttling, evaporation.
- ✅ **COP** for AC is the ratio of cooling effect to compressor work; **real automotive COP ≈ 3 – 4**.
- ✅ R-134a is being **replaced by R-1234yf** due to lower GWP (4 vs 1430).
- ✅ Demist logic: route **hot, dry air** to windshield — engage AC compressor even in winter for dehumidification.
- ✅ Dew-point approximation: $T_{dew} = T_{air} - (100 - RH)/5$.
- ✅ Cabin thermal load on occupant uses **convection + radiation + evaporation** — all three must be balanced.
- ✅ **NVH** = Noise, Vibration, Harshness; sources are powertrain, road, and aerodynamic.
- ✅ **Mass Law:** $STL = 20 \log(mf) - 47$; **6 dB per doubling** of mass or frequency.
- ✅ Cabin noise control strategy hierarchy: **Source → Path → Receiver**.
- ✅ A-weighting filters match human ear response; always quote dB(A) for perceived noise.
- ✅ **ANC (Active Noise Control)** uses destructive interference, effective for **50 – 250 Hz** low-frequency boom.
- ✅ Laminated windscreen (PVB interlayer) provides **STL > 30 dB** at speech frequencies.
- ✅ **Double-panel with air gap** breaks the Mass Law limit and adds the **coincidence-dip correction**.
- ✅ Multi-source SPL is **energy-additive, not arithmetic**: $L_{tot} = 10 \log \Sigma 10^{L_i/10}$.
- ✅ **Engine encapsulation** + **dash insulator** + **wheel-arch liner** are the three pillars of cabin quietness in a mid-segment car.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HVAC Systems and Airflow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** HVAC Systems and Airflow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, and Air-Conditioning)** is an integrated, microprocessor-controlled **Thermal Management Subsystem** of a passenger vehicle whose engineering objective is to establish and maintain a stable, healthy, and ergonomically optimized **micro-climate** inside the occupant cabin.

In KTU/SAE terminology, the cabin climate must simultaneously satisfy four distinct engineering targets:

1. **Thermal Comfort** — governed primarily by **Fanger's PMV (Predicted Mean Vote)** and **PPD (Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied)** model (ISO 7730 / ASHRAE 55).
2. **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** — particulate filtration, CO₂ dilution, and humidity regulation.
3. **Safety (Visibility)** — windshield **defrosting / demisting** within prescribed time limits (FMVSS 103 / AIS-011).
4. **Acoustic & Draft Comfort** — air velocity at occupant breathing zone must remain below **$\leq 0.25 \text{ m/s}$** in cooling mode to avoid the "draft sensation."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Note:** Ergonomics of HVAC is **NOT** about thermodynamics alone. It is the intersection of **heat transfer, fluid mechanics, human physiology, and control engineering.** Examiner questions frequently cross-link HVAC with **driver alertness, fatigue, and crash safety** (frosted windshield = zero visibility = crash risk).

---

### 1.2 The Four Functional Sub-Systems of an Automotive HVAC Unit

| # | Sub-System | Primary Ergonomic Function | Key Components |
|---|-----------|----------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Heating** | Counter cold-start driver discomfort, defrost windshield | Heater core, coolant hoses, blower |
| 2 | **Cooling (A/C)** | Reduce cabin temp below ambient | Compressor, condenser, evaporator, expansion valve |
| 3 | **Ventilation** | Fresh air intake, CO₂/humidity purge | Fresh/recirc damper, blower fan, ducts |
| 4 | **Air Distribution** | Direct airflow to face, feet, windshield | Mode doors, blend doors, registers |

> [!NOTE]
> The mnemonic **"HVAC = Health, Visibility, Alertness, Comfort"** is a popular KTU viva framing — remember it.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cabin as a Living Organ"

Imagine the car cabin is a **human lung wrapped inside a sweater**:

- The **HVAC box** is the **lungs** — it breathes fresh air in and stale air out.
- The **refrigerant circuit** is the **sweat glands** — it absorbs heat and expels it outside.
- The **heater core** is the **electric blanket** — it warms the air using the engine's own waste heat (a brilliant case of **thermal recovery** — no extra fuel is burnt for heating).
- The **blower fan** is the **diaphragm** — it forces the conditioned air through the ducts.
- The **mode doors** are the **facial muscles** — they decide where the air lands (face vs. feet vs. windshield).

This analogy is useful because it highlights the **ergonomic philosophy**: the HVAC system is not just an appliance bolted to the dashboard — it is an **organ system of the vehicle** that keeps the *human organ system* (the driver) functioning safely.

---

### 1.4 Key Industry Standards & Metrics (Must-Memorize)

| Standard | Full Name | Relevance |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **ISO 7730** | Ergonomics of the thermal environment | PMV/PPD comfort model |
| **ASHRAE 55** | Thermal Environmental Conditions for Human Occupancy | US counterpart of ISO 7730 |
| **SAE J276** | Procedure for Measuring System Refrigerant Mass | A/C charge verification |
| **FMVSS 103** | US Federal Defrosting/Defogging Performance | Windshield clarity in **$\leq 20$ min** at $-18^\circ\text{C}$ |
| **AIS-011** | Indian Automotive Industry Standard (equivalent) | Defrost test in Indian homologation |
| **ISO 14617** | Cabin air filter test methodology | Particulate filtration efficiency |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants the KTU paper expects you to know cold:**
> - Cabin set-point temperature range: **$20^\circ\text{C}$ to $26^\circ\text{C}$**
> - Acceptable humidity: **$30\%$ to $70\%$ RH**
> - Recommended air-change rate: **$\geq 7.5 \text{ L/s}$ per occupant** (ASHRAE 62.1)
> - CO₂ threshold for driver drowsiness onset: **> 1000 ppm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Vapor-Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — The Heart of A/C

The automotive air-conditioner is a **closed-loop vapor-compression refrigerator** with four irreversible-yet-near-ideal components:

1. **Compressor (1 → 2):** Low-pressure vapor is compressed to high-pressure, superheated vapor. Work input: $W_c$.
2. **Condenser (2 → 3):** Hot, high-pressure vapor rejects heat to ambient air (condenser fans + vehicle ram-air). Exits as saturated/sub-cooled liquid.
3. **Expansion Valve / Throttle (3 → 4):** Isenthalpic expansion — pressure drops, temperature drops. Two-phase mixture forms.
4. **Evaporator (4 → 1):** Low-pressure liquid absorbs heat from cabin air (forced over the evaporator coil by the blower). Exits as low-pressure vapor.

> [!NOTE]
> **The hidden ergonomic genius:** The evaporator *cools* the cabin air AND *dehumidifies* it simultaneously. As warm humid cabin air passes over the cold coil (typically **$0^\circ\text{C}$ to $5^\circ\text{C}$**), moisture condenses on the fins and drips away. This dehumidification is critical because high humidity makes occupants feel *hotter* than the dry-bulb temperature suggests (clammy skin, sweat that won't evaporate).

---

### 2.2 Heat Load on the Cabin — Where Does the Heat Come From?

The total cooling/heating load $Q_{total}$ on the HVAC system is the sum of **five heat-gain (or heat-loss) mechanisms**:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{conduction} + Q_{solar} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{internal} + Q_{ventilation}$$

Where each term has a distinct physical origin:

| Heat Term | Source | Engineering Mitigation |
|-----------|--------|------------------------|
| $Q_{conduction}$ | Heat transfer through glass, body panels, roof | IR-reflective glass, insulated headliner |
| $Q_{solar}$ | Direct + diffuse solar radiation (up to **$1000 \text{ W/m}^2$**) | Tinted glass, sunshades |
| $Q_{infiltration}$ | Uncontrolled air leakage through door/window seals | Better seal design |
| $Q_{internal}$ | Occupant metabolic heat (**$\approx 100 \text{ W}$ seated adult**), electronics | Cabin pre-conditioning |
| $Q_{ventilation}$ | Required fresh-air intake for CO₂ dilution | Heat-recovery ventilator (HEV/EV) |

---

### 2.3 The Heating Side — Free Waste Heat

In a conventional ICE vehicle, the heater core is supplied with **hot engine coolant** (typically **$85^\circ\text{C}$ to $105^\circ\text{C}$**). This is a brilliant example of **ergonomic energy recovery**: the cabin heating costs virtually **zero extra fuel** because the engine *must* shed this heat anyway through the radiator.

In **Battery Electric Vehicles (BEVs)**, however, no such waste-heat source exists, so a **Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC) heater** or a **heat-pump** must be used. This is why EV HVAC efficiency directly impacts **driving range** — a heavily loaded cabin heater can cut range by **$10\%$ to $30\%$.**

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in almost every Part B question.**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_{ref} = \dot{m}_r \,(h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration effect (heat absorbed in evaporator) | $\text{kW}$ |
| 2 | $W_c = \dot{m}_r \,(h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work input | $\text{kW}$ |
| 3 | $COP_{cooling} = \dfrac{Q_{ref}}{W_c} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance (A/C) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $COP_{heating} = COP_{cooling} + 1$ | Heat-pump COP (thermodynamic identity) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat transfer (air side) | $\text{W}$ |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, h_{fg}$ | Latent heat (dehumidification) | $\text{W}$ |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{\rho_{air} \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | Required airflow rate for given load | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 8 | $ACH = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{air}}{V_{cabin}} \times 3600$ | Air Changes per Hour | $\text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $PMV \approx f(M, I_{cl}, t_a, \bar{t}_r, v_{air}, p_a)$ | Fanger's PMV (6 ergonomic variables) | $-3$ to $+3$ |
| 10 | $PPD = 100 - 95 \, e^{-0.03353 \, PMV^4 - 0.2179 \, PMV^2}$ | Percentage dissatisfied from PMV | $\%\; (0\text{ to } 100)$ |
| 11 | $t_{dew} = t_{dry} - \dfrac{(100 - RH)}{5}$ | Approximate dew-point depression | $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| 12 | $\dot{m}_{fresh} = n_{occ} \times 7.5 \text{ L/s}$ | ASHRAE fresh-air requirement | $\text{L/s}$ |

> **Symbols:** $\dot{m}_r$ = refrigerant mass flow rate, $h$ = specific enthalpy, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\rho$ = density, $n_{occ}$ = number of occupants, $M$ = metabolic rate, $I_{cl}$ = clothing insulation (clo), $\bar{t}_r$ = mean radiant temperature, $v_{air}$ = air velocity, $p_a$ = water vapor pressure.

---

### 2.5 The PMV Model — Why It Is Ergonomics, Not Just HVAC

Fanger's **Predicted Mean Vote (PMV)** model is the **only** universally accepted thermal-comfort model and is directly testable in KTU exams because it has **six explicit input variables**, each of which the cabin HVAC must control:

$$PMV = \big[ 0.303 \, e^{-0.036 \, M} + 0.028 \big] \, L$$

where $L$ is the **thermal load on the body** defined as the imbalance between internal heat production and heat loss to the environment.

| PMV Value | Sensation | Ergonomic Interpretation |
|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|
| $+3$ | Very hot | Driver irritability, grip-force reduction |
| $+2$ | Warm | Mild sweat onset |
| $+1$ | Slightly warm | Optimal upper bound |
| $0$ | **Neutral** | **The KTU "Gold Standard"** |
| $-1$ | Slightly cool | Optimal lower bound |
| $-2$ | Cool | Cold fingers, reduced dexterity |
| $-3$ | Very cold | Severe reaction-time degradation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite question:** *"Explain why a PMV of zero is the design target and how cabin HVAC parameters are tuned to achieve it."* — Answer: it minimizes **PPD** (which has a minimum of $5\%$, not zero, because no population is perfectly uniform), maximizing driver comfort, alertness, and reaction time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cabin Airflow Rate from Heat-Load Equation

**Problem Statement:** A sedan cabin has a total sensible + latent cooling load of **$\dot{Q}_{total} = 4.2 \text{ kW}$** at design ambient. The blower draws cabin return air at **$32^\circ\text{C}$** and must deliver it to the occupants at **$18^\circ\text{C}$**. Compute the **required airflow rate** and the **Air Changes per Hour (ACH)**.

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_{total} = 4200 \text{ W}$
- $t_{return} = 32^\circ\text{C}$
- $t_{supply} = 18^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T = 32 - 18 = 14^\circ\text{C}$ (or **$14 \text{ K}$**, since these are temperature *differences*)
- $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ (standard atmospheric air)
- $\rho_{air} = 1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at $25^\circ\text{C}$, sea level)
- $V_{cabin} = 2.8 \text{ m}^3$ (typical 4-seater cabin)

**Step 1 — Start with the sensible-heat equation:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{air} \, c_p \, \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the mass flow rate of air:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{c_p \, \Delta T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{4200 \text{ W}}{1005 \text{ J/(kg·K)} \times 14 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$1005 \times 14 = 14070 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{4200}{14070} = 0.2985 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to volumetric flow rate using the ideal-gas relation $\dot{V} = \dot{m} / \rho$:**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{0.2985 \text{ kg/s}}{1.20 \text{ kg/m}^3} = 0.2488 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Express in cubic metres per hour (for the ACH formula):**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 0.2488 \times 3600 = 895.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Air Changes per Hour:**

$$ACH = \frac{\dot{V}_{air}}{V_{cabin}} = \frac{895.5}{2.8} = 319.8 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 9 — Sanity check & interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{air} \approx 0.249 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad ; \quad ACH \approx 320 \text{ hr}^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** A typical car cabin air volume is replaced **$\approx 320$ times per hour**. This is roughly **$8\times$ to $10\times$** higher than a residential building's ACH (which is around $30$ to $50 \text{ hr}^{-1}$). This extremely high ACH is necessary because the cabin is a *closed, sun-baked, occupant-dense, glass-heavy box* — a true thermal extreme environment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coefficient of Performance of the Automotive A/C Cycle

**Step 1 — Identify the four state points on a P-h (Pressure-Enthalpy) diagram:**
- State 1: Saturated vapor at evaporator pressure
- State 2: Superheated vapor at condenser pressure (after compressor)
- State 3: Saturated liquid at condenser pressure (after condenser)
- State 4: Two-phase mixture at evaporator pressure (after expansion valve)

**Step 2 — Apply steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the compressor (neglecting KE/PE):**

$$\dot{W}_c = \dot{m}_r \,(h_2 - h_1)$$

**Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the evaporator (no work, no heat loss to surroundings other than cabin):**

$$\dot{Q}_{evap} = \dot{m}_r \,(h_1 - h_4)$$

**Step 4 — By definition, the COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of desired effect to work input:**

$$COP_{R} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{evap}}{\dot{W}_c}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the SFEE expressions:**

$$COP_{R} = \frac{\dot{m}_r \,(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}_r \,(h_2 - h_1)} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form (used in every KTU problem):**

$$\boxed{COP_{R} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

**Step 7 — Numerical example with refrigerant R-134a:**

| State | Enthalpy $h$ (kJ/kg) | Location |
|-------|----------------------|----------|
| 1 | $h_1 = 247.32$ | Compressor inlet (sat. vapor, 0.18 MPa) |
| 2 | $h_2 = 271.71$ | Compressor outlet (superheated, 0.9 MPa) |
| 3 | $h_3 = 101.61$ | Condenser outlet (sat. liquid, 0.9 MPa) |
| 4 | $h_4 = 101.61$ | Evaporator inlet (isenthalpic, 0.18 MPa) |

$$COP_{R} = \frac{247.32 - 101.61}{271.71 - 247.32} = \frac{145.71}{24.39} \approx 5.97$$

> [!NOTE]
> A COP of $\approx 6$ means **for every 1 kW of compressor work, the system removes $\approx 6$ kW of heat** from the cabin. This is why an automotive A/C is far more "efficient" than resistive heating — it *moves* heat rather than *generates* it.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Dehumidification (Latent Heat) Capacity of the Evaporator

**Step 1 — The evaporator coil surface temperature $t_{coil}$ is held below the cabin air dew point so that moisture condenses:**

$$t_{coil} \leq t_{dew, cabin}$$

**Step 2 — The cabin dew point is computed from the psychrometric identity:**

$$t_{dew} = t_{dry} - \frac{(100 - RH)}{5}$$

**Step 3 — At $t_{cabin} = 30^\circ\text{C}$ and $RH = 60\%$, the dew point is:**

$$t_{dew} = 30 - \frac{100 - 60}{5} = 30 - 8 = 22^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Therefore the evaporator coil must be held at or below $22^\circ\text{C}$ — typically controlled at $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $5^\circ\text{C}$ for margin.**

**Step 5 — The mass of water removed per second equals the latent-heat balance:**

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{latent}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{latent}}{2450 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergonomic link:** Removing $\approx 0.3 \text{ kg/hr}$ of moisture from a 4-occupant cabin is the difference between a "sticky-shirt" drive and a comfortable one. Dehumidification is a *hidden* but critical HVAC ergonomic function.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Automotive HVAC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Cabin Air Return] --> B[Blower Fan]
    B --> C[Evaporator Coil]
    C --> D[Heater Core]
    D --> E[Blend Air Door]
    E --> F[Mode Doors]
    F --> G[Face Vents]
    F --> H[Foot Vents]
    F --> I[Defrost Vents]
    J[Fresh Air Intake] --> K[Recirculation Door]
    K --> B
    L[Engine Coolant In] --> D
    M[Engine Coolant Out] --> D
    N[Refrigerant Loop] --> C
    O[Cabin Filter] --> K
    P[Climate Control ECU] --> Q[Actuators]
    Q --> E
    Q --> F
    Q --> K
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) with State Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[State 1: Sat Vapor Low P] --> S2[State 2: Superheated High P]
    S2 --> S3[State 3: Sat Liquid High P]
    S3 --> S4[State 4: Two Phase Low P]
    S4 --> S1
    S1 -.Compressor.-> S2
    S2 -.Condenser Ambient Heat Rejection.-> S3
    S3 -.Expansion Valve Isenthalpic.-> S4
    S4 -.Evaporator Cabin Heat Absorption.-> S1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Airflow Pattern Inside Cabin (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cabin
        direction LR
        DF[Dashboard Face Vents] --> OCC[Occupant Breathing Zone]
        FT[Floor Foot Vents] --> OCC
        WS[Windshield Defrost Vents] --> WS2[Windshield Glass]
        OCC --> EX[Exhaust Stale Air Vents]
    end
    EXT[External Fresh Air] --> FL[Cabin Air Filter] --> DF
    REC[Recirculated Cabin Air] --> DF
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Ergonomic Control Loop (Climate ECU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cabin Temp Sensor] --> ECU[Climate ECU]
    B[Ambient Temp Sensor] --> ECU
    C[Humidity Sensor] --> ECU
    D[Solar Sensor] --> ECU
    E[Driver Setpoint Knob] --> ECU
    ECU --> F[Compressor Clutch Relay]
    ECU --> G[Blower Speed PWM]
    ECU --> H[Blend Door Actuator]
    ECU --> I[Mode Door Actuator]
    ECU --> J[Recirc Door Actuator]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture** is used here (rather than a free-body physical drawing) because the actual physical layout varies enormously across vehicle platforms, but the *functional* signal-flow and component-interaction logic is universal — exactly what KTU examiners test.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model] (3 Marks)**
**"Define PMV and PPD as per ISO 7730. Why is a PMV of 0 the design target even though it does not give 0% dissatisfaction?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~80 words):**

- **PMV (Predicted Mean Vote):** An index that predicts the mean thermal sensation vote (on a scale from $-3$ cold to $+3$ hot) of a large group of occupants exposed to a given thermal environment, as derived by Fanger (1970). **1 mark**
- **PPD (Predicted Percentage Dissatisfied):** The percentage of occupants predicted to feel dissatisfied (i.e., vote $\pm 2$ or $\pm 3$) at a given PMV value. **1 mark**
- **Why PMV=0 is the target:** PPD has a mathematical minimum of $5\%$ even at PMV=0, due to inter-individual physiological variation. PMV=0 gives the *lowest achievable* PPD, hence the optimum comfort target. **1 mark**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model] (3 Marks)**
**"List the four functional sub-systems of an automotive HVAC unit and state the primary ergonomic purpose of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks, ~70 words):**

1. **Heating** — Maintains cabin temp above ambient using engine coolant waste heat; prevents cold-induced dexterity loss. **0.75 mark**
2. **Cooling (A/C)** — Lowers cabin temp and dehumidifies air using VCRC; prevents heat-induced fatigue and sweating. **0.75 mark**
3. **Ventilation** — Introduces filtered fresh air at $\geq 7.5 \text{ L/s}$ per occupant; controls CO₂ below 1000 ppm to avoid drowsiness. **0.75 mark**
4. **Air Distribution** — Routes conditioned air to face/feet/windshield; satisfies local thermal preferences and defrosts windshield. **0.75 mark**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A [14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an automotive **Vapor Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**. Label all four state points and indicate enthalpy changes. **7 marks**

**(b)** A sedan cabin has a cooling load of **$5.5 \text{ kW}$**. The blower takes air at **$35^\circ\text{C}$** and delivers it at **$15^\circ\text{C}$**. Compute the required **mass flow rate, volumetric flow rate, and ACH** for a cabin volume of **$3.0 \text{ m}^3$**. Take $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$ and $\rho = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) [7 marks] Block diagram + working:**

**Working (4 marks):** The cycle has four processes on a **P-h diagram**:
- **1 → 2 (Compressor):** Low-pressure saturated vapor compressed to high-pressure superheated vapor. Refrigerant temperature rises. **Compressor work $W_c = h_2 - h_1$.** [1 mark]
- **2 → 3 (Condenser):** Hot vapor rejects heat to ambient (atmospheric air blown by ram-air and condenser fan) and condenses to saturated liquid. **Heat rejected $Q_{cond} = h_2 - h_3$.** [1 mark]
- **3 → 4 (Expansion Valve):** Isenthalpic throttling — high-pressure liquid expands to low-pressure two-phase mixture. **$h_4 = h_3$.** [1 mark]
- **4 → 1 (Evaporator):** Two-phase mixture absorbs heat from cabin air (forced by blower), evaporating to saturated vapor. **Refrigeration effect $Q_{ref} = h_1 - h_4$.** [1 mark]

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Use the VCRC mermaid diagram from Section 4.2 (or draw the P-h chart).

**Ergonomic Linkage (1 mark):** This cycle enables **rapid cabin cool-down** (driver hand on steering is comfortable within 5–10 min) and **simultaneous dehumidification** (fog-free windshield), both critical safety/ergonomic outcomes.

---

**(b) [7 marks] Step-by-step numerical solution:**

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_{total} = 5500 \text{ W}$
- $t_{return} = 35^\circ\text{C}$, $t_{supply} = 15^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T = 35 - 15 = 20^\circ\text{C} = 20 \text{ K}$
- $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $\rho = 1.18 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $V_{cabin} = 3.0 \text{ m}^3$

**Step 1 — Sensible heat equation:**

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{m}$:**

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{5500}{1005 \times 20} = \frac{5500}{20100} = 0.2736 \text{ kg/s} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\rho} = \frac{0.2736}{1.18} = 0.2319 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to per hour:**

$$\dot{V}_{air} = 0.2319 \times 3600 = 834.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 5 — Air Changes per Hour:**

$$ACH = \frac{834.7}{3.0} = 278.2 \text{ hr}^{-1} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{air} \approx 0.274 \text{ kg/s} \quad ; \quad \dot{V}_{air} \approx 0.232 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \quad ; \quad ACH \approx 278 \text{ hr}^{-1}}$$

[1 mark for correct conclusion that the cabin air is recycled about **278 times per hour** — well above the ASHRAE residential guideline of $30\text{–}50 \text{ hr}^{-1}$, justifying the high blower capacity required.]

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain **Fanger's PMV model** in detail. List the **six variables** that determine PMV and describe how an automotive climate-control system uses sensors to measure each one. **7 marks**

**(b)** A vehicle is parked in direct sun at **$42^\circ\text{C}$ ambient** with **$70\%$ RH**. The cabin reaches **$58^\circ\text{C}$** before the driver enters. The A/C system pulls this cabin air down to a comfortable **$24^\circ\text{C}$** at the supply vent. The total latent load is **$0.8 \text{ kW}$** due to the high humidity. Compute: **(i)** the sensible cooling load, and **(ii)** the mass of water condensed per hour in the evaporator. Take $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$ and $h_{fg} = 2450 \text{ kJ/kg}$. Assume the blower supplies **$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.30 \text{ kg/s}$**. **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) [7 marks] Fanger's PMV model:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fanger's **Predicted Mean Vote (PMV)** is an index predicting the mean thermal sensation of a large population exposed to a specific thermal environment, derived from the body's **heat balance equation** (Fanger, 1970). PMV ranges from $-3$ (very cold) through $0$ (neutral) to $+3$ (very hot).

**The Six Variables (3 marks):**

| # | Variable | Symbol | Typical Cabin Value | Sensor in Vehicle |
|---|----------|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Metabolic rate | $M$ | **$70\text{–}100 \text{ W}$** (seated, light activity) | Estimated from seat-belt buckle + driver-presence sensor |
| 2 | Clothing insulation | $I_{cl}$ | **$0.7\text{–}1.0 \text{ clo}$** (business suit to winter coat) | Driver-selected via HMI menu |
| 3 | Air temperature | $t_a$ | **$20\text{–}26^\circ\text{C}$** | In-cabin NTC thermistor |
| 4 | Mean radiant temperature | $\bar{t}_r$ | Influenced by glass & body panel temps | Solar sensor + black-globe equivalent algorithm |
| 5 | Air velocity | $v_{air}$ | **$\leq 0.25 \text{ m/s}$** | Blower PWM duty feedback |
| 6 | Water vapor pressure | $p_a$ | **$\leq 1.5 \text{ kPa}$** | Humidity sensor (capacitive) |

**Climate-Control System Response (2 marks):** The **Climate Control ECU** continuously reads the in-cabin temperature, ambient temperature, humidity, and solar-load sensors, compares with the driver setpoint, and actuates the **blend door, mode doors, blower speed, compressor clutch, and recirculation door** to drive PMV toward **$0$**. Modern systems use **fuzzy-logic or model-predictive controllers** to anticipate thermal transients (e.g., entering a tunnel).

---

**(b) [7 marks] Numerical solution:**

**Given:**
- $\Delta T = 58 - 24 = 34^\circ\text{C} = 34 \text{ K}$
- $\dot{Q}_{latent} = 0.8 \text{ kW} = 800 \text{ W}$
- $\dot{m}_{air} = 0.30 \text{ kg/s}$
- $c_p = 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $h_{fg} = 2450 \text{ kJ/kg}$

**(i) Sensible cooling load (4 marks):**

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = 0.30 \times 1005 \times 34$$

$$\dot{Q}_{sensible} = 10251 \text{ W} \approx 10.25 \text{ kW} \quad \text{[3 marks for steps, 1 mark for answer]}$$

**(ii) Mass of water condensed per hour (3 marks):**

From latent heat balance: $\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot h_{fg}$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{latent}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{800 \text{ W}}{2450 \times 10^3 \text{ J/kg}} = 3.265 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to per hour:

$$\dot{m}_{water} = 3.265 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.176 \text{ kg/hr} \quad \text{[2 marks for steps, 1 mark for answer]}$$

**Final Boxed Answer:**

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{sensible} \approx 10.25 \text{ kW} \quad ; \quad \dot{m}_{water} \approx 1.18 \text{ kg/hr}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Note how the **latent load (0.8 kW) is dwarfed by the sensible load (10.25 kW)** when the cabin is sun-soaked. This is why the *first* priority of a hot-soak recovery system is to drop the air temperature (sensible) before the system can even begin to dehumidify effectively.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes in HVAC questions (as per KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $\Delta T$ in $^\circ\text{C}$ then plugging into an equation that requires Kelvin. *Fix:* For a *temperature difference*, $^\circ\text{C}$ and $\text{K}$ are numerically identical — but examiners still expect the symbol $\text{K}$ or a clarifying note. **−0.5 mark** if ambiguous.
> 2. **Confusing sensible vs. latent load:** $mc_p\Delta T$ is **sensible only**; dehumidification uses $h_{fg}$. Mixing them up costs **−1 mark** minimum.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert seconds to hours** (or kg/s to kg/hr) in the final answer. **−0.5 mark** for unit error.
> 4. **Skipping the ergonomic "interpretation" sentence:** KTU 2024 explicitly tests OBE-style "engineering relevance." A pure numerical answer with no ergonomic statement loses **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Using $R-12$ data instead of $R-134a$** (modern KTU papers use R-134a or R-1234yf). Carrying forward outdated refrigerant properties is a **silent −0.5 mark** deduction.
> 6. **Stating "COP = 1" or "COP = 100%" for the A/C:** A/C is a refrigerator, NOT a heat engine. Its COP is greater than 1 (typically **3 to 6**). Writing "efficiency" instead of "COP" is a mark-losing terminology error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Automotive HVAC = 4 sub-systems:** Heating, Cooling (A/C), Ventilation, Air Distribution. [Must-memorize]
- **The cooling cycle is VCRC** with 4 state points, governed by $COP_R = (h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$.
- **Heating uses FREE waste heat** from engine coolant in ICE vehicles; EVs need a PTC heater or heat pump.
- **PMV target = 0** gives minimum PPD = **5%** (Fanger's identity, ISO 7730).
- **Six PMV inputs:** $M$, $I_{cl}$, $t_a$, $\bar{t}_r$, $v_{air}$, $p_a$ — all sensor-monitored in modern vehicles.
- **Air-change rate** is much higher in cars ($\sim 300 \text{ hr}^{-1}$) than in buildings ($\sim 30\text{–}50 \text{ hr}^{-1}$).
- **Fresh air per occupant:** ASHRAE specifies **$\geq 7.5 \text{ L/s}$**; CO₂ must stay **< 1000 ppm** to avoid drowsiness.
- **Sensible load formula:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ — note units carefully.
- **Latent load formula:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{water} \cdot h_{fg}$ — $h_{fg} \approx 2450 \text{ kJ/kg}$ for water.
- **Evaporator coil temperature** must stay **below dew point** to dehumidify.
- **Defrosting** is a safety-critical function (FMVSS 103 / AIS-011) — clear windshield in **$\leq 20$ min** at $-18^\circ\text{C}$.
- **Draft threshold:** airflow at breathing zone **$\leq 0.25 \text{ m/s}$** to avoid discomfort.
- **Cabin filter** protects IAQ; replace every **$15{,}000\text{–}20{,}000 \text{ km}$**.
- **Refrigerant evolution:** R-12 (banned) → R-134a (current) → R-1234yf (future, low-GWP).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH) Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Noise, Vibration, and Harshness (NVH)** is the collective discipline in automotive engineering that deals with the *generation*, *transmission*, and *reception* of unwanted sound and mechanical oscillations inside and around a vehicle. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (OEMUT722), NVH is treated as the umbrella term that integrates three closely coupled sub-phenomena:

- **Noise** — undesirable acoustic pressure fluctuations in air (typically 20 Hz – 20 kHz) radiated by a vehicle subsystem and perceived by the human ear.
- **Vibration** — time-dependent oscillatory motion of a structure (typically 0.5 Hz – 500 Hz in automotive contexts) measured as displacement, velocity, or acceleration.
- **Harshness** — the *subjective human perception* of the combined noise and vibration environment, judged by the vehicle occupants in terms of ride comfort, sound quality, and tactile feel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis (Module 2):** Students must understand NVH as a *system-level* problem. The syllabus expects that the candidate can identify sources (engine, powertrain, tire-road, aerodynamic, structural), describe propagation paths (airborne vs structure-borne), and prescribe control strategies (passive damping, isolation, absorption, and active control).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Boiling Kettle" Picture

Imagine you are sitting inside a quiet room and someone starts boiling a kettle in the next room. Three things happen at once:

1. You **hear** a hissing whistle (this is *Noise* — airborne sound travelling through air).
2. If you place your hand on the shared wall, you **feel** a slight buzz (this is *Vibration* — mechanical oscillation travelling through the structure).
3. Your brain instantly decides whether the buzz is *annoying* (this is *Harshness* — the human judgement, or "psychoacoustic" reaction).

In a car, the engine is the "kettle," the body structure and air cavity are the "wall and room," and the driver's seat, steering wheel, and ears are the "sensors." NVH control is simply the engineering effort to calm the kettle, thicken the wall, and silence the room — all at the same time.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Reference Constants

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard reference values** must be memorized — they appear verbatim in KTU numerical problems.

| Quantity | Symbol | Reference Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference sound pressure | $p_0$ | **20 μPa** | Pascal |
| Reference sound intensity | $I_0$ | **10⁻¹²** | W/m² |
| Reference sound power | $W_0$ | **10⁻¹²** | W |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $p_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa** | kPa |
| Speed of sound in air (20 °C) | $c$ | **343 m/s** | m/s |
| Air density (20 °C) | $\rho$ | **1.204 kg/m³** | kg/m³ |
| Reference acceleration (vibration) | $a_0$ | **10⁻⁶** | m/s² |
| Standard human hearing threshold | $L_{p,th}$ | **0 dB** | dB |

### 1.4 Why NVH is Central to Automotive Ergonomics

NVH sits at the exact intersection of *engineering physics* and *human comfort*. A vehicle can be mechanically perfect but ergonomically unacceptable if NVH is poor. OEMUT722 treats NVH as one of the three pillars of cabin comfort, alongside thermal management and seating ergonomics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of NVH: Detailed Taxonomy

- **Sources** (excitation points): engine combustion, gearbox meshing, tire-road interaction, wind turbulence, brake squeal, HVAC fans.
- **Path** (transmission medium): air (airborne noise), structural members (structure-borne vibration), hydraulic mounts (fluid-borne).
- **Receiver** (response point): driver's ear (noise), hands on steering (vibration), seat pan (whole-body vibration), feet on floor (low-frequency boom).

### 2.2 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) — The dB Framework

Sound pressure is a tiny fluctuating pressure on top of atmospheric pressure. Because the human ear responds logarithmically, a logarithmic scale called the **decibel (dB)** is used.

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB SPL]}$$

where $p_{rms}$ is the root-mean-square sound pressure and $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

**Why 20 log₁₀?** Sound *intensity* is proportional to $p^2$, and $10 \log_{10}(p^2/p_0^2) = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$. Hence the doubling of the coefficient.

### 2.3 Sound Power Level vs Sound Intensity Level

- **Sound power $W$** (Watt) is a *property of the source* — independent of distance.
- **Sound intensity $I$** (W/m²) is power per unit area at a point.
- **Sound pressure $p$** (Pa) is the local fluctuating pressure at a point.

$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right), \qquad L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$$

For a free-field point source radiating uniformly into a hemisphere (a typical in-car or near-road assumption):

$$I = \dfrac{W}{2\pi r^2}$$

### 2.4 Addition of Two Independent Sound Sources

A very common KTU question: *Two machines produce 80 dB and 83 dB separately. What is the combined level?*

$$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$

**Practical engineering rule:** If two sources differ by more than **10 dB**, the quieter one contributes less than 0.5 dB and can be ignored.

### 2.5 A-Weighting — Mimicking the Human Ear

The ear is not equally sensitive at all frequencies. **A-weighting** is a standard filter (IEC 61672) that de-emphasizes very low and very high frequencies, mimicking average human perception.

$$L_{A} = L_p + \Delta A(f) \quad \text{[dB(A)]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often quote road-traffic noise limits in **dB(A)**, e.g., 75 dB(A) at 7.5 m for new passenger cars (Indian AIS-008 / ECE R51 type approval).

### 2.6 Vibration Transmissibility — The Heart of Vibration Isolation

When a machine is mounted on isolators, the force transmitted to the foundation is reduced. **Transmissibility $T$** is the ratio of transmitted force amplitude $F_T$ to exciting force amplitude $F_0$.

$$T = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

where:
- $\zeta$ = **damping ratio** (dimensionless, $0 < \zeta < 1$ for typical elastomeric mounts).
- $r = \omega / \omega_n$ = **frequency ratio** (forcing frequency divided by natural frequency).
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ = **undamped natural angular frequency** (rad/s).
- $f_n = \omega_n / (2\pi)$ = **natural frequency** (Hz).

**Key design rule:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective isolation requires $r > \sqrt{2}$.** Above this threshold, $T < 1$ and continues to fall. Designers therefore choose a soft mount such that $f_n$ is well below the lowest excitation frequency (e.g., engine idle firing frequency).

For high $r$ (and low $\zeta$):

$$T \approx \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1} \approx \dfrac{1}{r^2} \quad \text{(for } r \gg 1 \text{)}$$

This means that *each doubling of the forcing frequency reduces the transmitted vibration by a factor of 4*, i.e., **12 dB** in vibration level.

### 2.7 Damping Ratio and Logarithmic Decrement

For a single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) system:

$$\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$$

where $c$ is the viscous damping coefficient and $c_c$ is the critical damping.

In practice damping is often measured from free-decay tests using the **logarithmic decrement** $\delta$:

$$\delta = \dfrac{1}{n}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x_i}{x_{i+n}}\right) \approx 2\pi\zeta \quad \text{(for small } \zeta \text{)}$$

### 2.8 Mass Law for Sound Transmission Loss (Single Wall)

For a thin, limp, single-panel partition of surface density $m_s$ (kg/m²) at frequency $f$ (Hz), the **sound transmission loss** $TL$ is given by the **mass law**:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m_s \cdot f) - 47 \quad \text{[dB]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **−47** constant arises from $20 \log_{10}(\rho_0 c / \pi)$ with $\rho_0 c \approx 413$ rayl in air. Each *doubling* of frequency or mass adds **6 dB** of TL.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Variable | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ | Sound pressure level | dB |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound power level | dB |
| 3 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Sound intensity level | dB |
| 4 | $L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ | dB addition | dB |
| 5 | $I = p_{rms}^2 / (\rho c)$ | Acoustic intensity (plane wave) | W/m² |
| 6 | $I = W / (2\pi r^2)$ | Intensity from point source (hemisphere) | W/m² |
| 7 | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(m_s f) - 47$ | Mass law for transmission loss | dB |
| 8 | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency of SDOF | Hz |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural angular frequency | rad/s |
| 10 | $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$ | Damping ratio | — |
| 11 | $T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | Vibration transmissibility | — |
| 12 | $r = \omega / \omega_n$ | Frequency ratio | — |
| 13 | $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | Logarithmic decrement approximation | — |
| 14 | $L_v = 20 \log_{10}(v/v_0)$ | Vibration velocity level ($v_0 = 10^{-9}$ m/s) | dB |
| 15 | $L_a = 20 \log_{10}(a/a_0)$ | Vibration acceleration level ($a_0 = 10^{-6}$ m/s²) | dB |
| 16 | $\text{STL} \propto \sqrt{f}$ for limp panel | Stiffness-controlled region | — |
| 17 | $\text{STL} \propto f^2$ for stiff panel | Coincidence-controlled region | — |
| 18 | $\eta_{damping} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{2\pi W}$ | Loss factor (damping metric) | — |

### 2.10 Real-World Utility

- **OEM NVH teams** use the transmissibility equation to select engine mounts: a typical passenger-car engine has a 4-cylinder idle firing frequency near 25 Hz, so mounts are designed with $f_n \approx 8$–$10$ Hz, giving $r \approx 2.5$–$3$ and a transmission loss of ≈ 12–15 dB.
- **Acoustic headliners and dash insulators** in cars are sized using the mass law — heavier pads (≈ 1.5–3 kg/m²) are used in premium vehicles to push transmission loss above 30 dB at speech frequencies.
- **Sound-quality engineering** uses A-weighted metrics and psychoacoustic models (Zwicker loudness, sharpness, roughness) to judge "harshness" subjectively.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Vibration Transmissibility for an SDOF Base-Excited System

**Starting physical model:** A mass $m$ is connected to a moving base by a spring $k$ and a damper $c$ in parallel. The base displacement is $y(t) = Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$. The mass absolute displacement is $x(t)$.

**Equation of motion** (Newton's second law, relative coordinate $z = x - y$):

$$m\ddot{x} + c(\dot{x} - \dot{y}) + k(x - y) = 0$$

Substitute $z = x - y \Rightarrow \ddot{z} = \ddot{x} - \ddot{y}$:

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}$$

Since $\ddot{y} = -\omega^2 Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the right-hand side is $m\omega^2 Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$. Assume steady-state harmonic response $z(t) = Z_0 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$(-m\omega^2 + k)Z_0 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + c\omega Z_0 \cos(\omega t - \phi) = m\omega^2 Y_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

Using phasor form (replace sin/cos with complex exponentials) the amplitude ratio becomes:

$$\dfrac{Z_0}{Y_0} = \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

The **transmitted force** to the mass is $F_T = k z + c \dot z$. Its amplitude relative to the excitation force $F_0 = m \omega^2 Y_0$:

$$\dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{m\omega^2}\cdot \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Now substitute $k = m\omega_n^2$ and $c = 2\zeta m \omega_n$:

$$\dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{m^2\omega_n^4 + 4\zeta^2 m^2 \omega_n^2 \omega^2}{m^2 \omega^4}}\cdot \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Factor out $m^2 \omega_n^4$ from the numerator inside the square root and divide:

$$\dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{r^4}}\cdot \dfrac{r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Combining the $r$ terms:

$$T = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

This is the standard textbook transmissibility. **End of derivation.** The result is the cornerstone of all vibration-isolation design in automotive mounts.

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Law for Sound Transmission Loss (Thin Limp Panel)

Consider an infinite, limp (zero bending stiffness) partition of surface density $m_s$ separating two regions. An incident plane wave of rms pressure $p_i$ strikes the panel; the panel moves and re-radiates a transmitted pressure $p_t$ on the other side. The transmission coefficient $\tau$ is defined as the ratio of transmitted to incident sound **intensity**:

$$\tau = \dfrac{I_t}{I_i}$$

For a mass-controlled partition, the panel behaves like a rigid mass moving under the action of the pressure difference. Its equation of motion gives a volume-velocity $q = v \cdot A$, where $v$ is panel velocity. The acoustic impedance seen by the panel is $Z_a = \rho c$ on each side, and the radiated intensity is $I_t = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho c v^2$.

Combining the mass equation with the boundary conditions:

$$\tau = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\omega m_s}{2\rho c}\right)^2}$$

For the typical engineering range where $\omega m_s \gg 2\rho c$ (the panel is *acoustically heavy*):

$$\tau \approx \left(\dfrac{2\rho c}{\omega m_s}\right)^2$$

The **transmission loss** is defined as:

$$TL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\tau}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\omega m_s}{2\rho c}\right)$$

Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi f \cdot m_s}{2\rho c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi f m_s}{\rho c}\right)$$

Using $\rho c \approx 413$ rayl at 20 °C:

$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m_s) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{413}{\pi}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m_s) - 20 \log_{10}(131.5)$$

$20 \log_{10}(131.5) \approx 42.4$. The widely quoted engineering approximation is therefore:

$$TL \approx 20 \log_{10}(f \cdot m_s) - 47 \quad \text{[dB]}$$

(The −47 constant is a rounded, internationally accepted approximation; the exact value depends on temperature and the convention for referencing.) This is the famous **mass law** — doubling mass or frequency adds ≈ 6 dB of isolation.

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke engine runs at 1500 rpm. Each engine mount is to be designed such that the vibration transmissibility at the engine firing frequency is less than 0.10. The mount stiffness is 220 kN/m and the engine mass on each mount is 35 kg. Assume damping ratio $\zeta = 0.08$. Determine:
(a) the firing frequency and the frequency ratio $r$,
(b) the required natural frequency and the corresponding spring stiffness to be redesigned if $T < 0.10$ is not satisfied,
(c) the dB reduction achieved at the operating point.

**Solution.**

**Step 1 — Firing frequency.**
For a 4-stroke engine, the firing frequency is the number of power strokes per second:

$$f_{fire} = \dfrac{N \cdot n_{cyl}}{2 \cdot 60} = \dfrac{1500 \cdot 4}{120} = 50 \ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Natural frequency of the given mount.**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{220 \times 10^3}{35}} = \sqrt{6285.7} \approx 79.28 \ \text{rad/s}$$

$$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{79.28}{2\pi} \approx 12.62 \ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency ratio.**

$$r = \dfrac{f_{fire}}{f_n} = \dfrac{50}{12.62} \approx 3.96$$

**Step 4 — Transmissibility at $r = 3.96$, $\zeta = 0.08$.**

$$T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 + (2 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 3.96)^2}{(1 - 3.96^2)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.08 \cdot 3.96)^2}}$$

Compute step by step:
- $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.08 \times 3.96 = 0.6336$.
- $(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.4015$.
- $r^2 = 15.68$; $1 - r^2 = -14.68$; $(1 - r^2)^2 = 215.5$.
- Numerator: $1 + 0.4015 = 1.4015$.
- Denominator: $215.5 + 0.4015 = 215.9$.

$$T = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.4015}{215.9}} = \sqrt{0.006491} = 0.0806$$

**Step 5 — Check requirement.** $T = 0.0806 < 0.10$ ✓ The mount is acceptable.

**Step 6 — dB reduction in vibration level.**

$$\Delta L_v = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(12.41) \approx 21.87 \ \text{dB}$$

So the mount reduces the vibration level at the firing frequency by ≈ **21.9 dB**.

**Step 7 — Sanity check using the high-$r$ approximation.**

$$T \approx \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1} = \dfrac{1}{15.68 - 1} = \dfrac{1}{14.68} = 0.0681$$

This is conservative but of the same order — the more accurate value of 0.0806 includes the small damping contribution.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Design Decisions

| Symptom | Likely Source | Path | Control Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Engine boom at 50–80 Hz | 4-cyl firing | Structure-borne | Soft engine mount (low $f_n$) |
| Road noise at 200–500 Hz | Tire tread impact | Airborne + structure | Mass-loaded dash insulator, laminated glass |
| Wind whistle at 1000+ Hz | A-pillar vortex | Airborne | Sealing, A-pillar trim, fine surface finishing |
| Gear whine at 1500+ Hz | Gear meshing | Structure + airborne | Tighter gear tolerances, NVH-optimised oils, Helmholtz resonator in transmission tunnel |
| Brake squeal > 1 kHz | Pad-disc friction | Structure-borne | Damping shims, chamfered pad edges, grease |
| Idle shake at cab | Engine imbalance | Structure-borne | Tuned mass damper on steering column |
| HVAC blower whine | Fan blade pass freq | Airborne | Duct lining, Helmholtz resonator in HVAC box |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 NVH System Block Diagram (Source–Path–Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[ENGcombustion\nExcitation Source] --> path1[Solid Mount\nStructure borne Path]
    src2[TIRE Road\nExcitation Source] --> path2[Air Cavity\nAirborne Path]
    src3[GEAR MESH\nExcitation Source] --> path3[Body Panels\nStructure Path]
    src4[AERO Flow\nExcitation Source] --> path4[Door Glass\nAirborne Path]
    path1 --> rcv1[Driver Ear\nAcoustic Receiver]
    path2 --> rcv1
    path3 --> rcv1
    path4 --> rcv1
    path1 --> rcv2[Steering Column\nVibration Receiver]
    path3 --> rcv2
    path1 --> rcv3[Seat Pan\nWhole Body Receiver]
    path3 --> rcv3
    rcv1 --> brain[Human Perception\nHarshness]
    rcv2 --> brain
    rcv3 --> brain
```

### 4.2 Transmissibility Curve (Behavioural Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    lowR["Low Frequency Region\nr less than sqrt 2\nT greater than 1\nVibration Amplified"]
    resonance["Resonance Peak\nr equals 1\nT can exceed 5 to 10\nCritical Damping Needed"]
    isolation["Isolation Region\nr greater than sqrt 2\nT less than 1\nSoft Mount Effective"]
    highR["High Frequency Region\nr much greater than 1\nT approx 1 over r squared\nEach Octave adds 12 dB"]
    lowR --> resonance --> isolation --> highR
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram of an Active Noise Control (ANC) System in a Vehicle Cabin

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    noise[Road and Engine Noise\nDisturbance Signal] --> mic[Error Microphone\nInside Cabin]
    mic --> ctrl[Adaptive Controller\nFXLMS or FxLMS]
    ref[Reference Sensor\nAccelerometer on Body] --> ctrl
    ctrl --> spk[Anti Noise Loudspeaker\nMounted in Headliner]
    spk --> cancel[Acoustic Cancellation\nAt Driver Ear Position]
    cancel --> ear[Driver Ear\nReduced SPL]
```

### 4.4 Damping Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start[NVH Problem Detected\nFrequency and Amplitude Identified] --> q1{Dominant Path}
    q1 -- Structure borne --> isol[Increase Isolation\nLower Mount Stiffness]
    q1 -- Airborne --> absorb[Add Absorption\nFoam or Fibre]
    q1 -- Resonance --> damp[Add Damping\nViscoelastic Layer]
    isol --> check[Re evaluate NVH Level\nAt Receiver]
    absorb --> check
    damp --> check
    check -- Not Acceptable --> active[Switch to Active Control\nANC or Active Mount]
    check -- Acceptable --> done[Design Locked]
    active --> check
```

### 4.5 Sound Transmission Loss vs Frequency (Mass-Law Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    lowF["Low Frequency\nBelow Coincidence\nMass Law Region\nTL grows 6 dB per Octave"]
    cof["Coincidence Frequency\nDip in TL\nCritical Design Point"]
    midF["Mid Frequency\nStiffness Controlled\nTL grows 12 dB per Octave"]
    highF["High Frequency\nDamping Controlled\nPlateau Region"]
    lowF --> cof --> midF --> highF
```

### 4.6 Vehicle NVH Source Map (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    powertrain[Powertrain Subsystem\nEngine Gearbox Mounts] --> bodySys
    tireSys[Tire Road Subsystem\nSuspension Bushes] --> bodySys
    aeroSys[Aerodynamic Subsystem\nMirror A pillar] --> bodySys
    bodySys[Body in White Structure\nPanels and Glazing] --> cabinSys[Cabin Air Cavity]
    cabinSys --> occupants[Driver and Passengers\nEar Hand Seat Foot]
    powertrain --> chassisSys[Chassis Frame\nSub frames]
    chassisSys --> cabinSys
```
