> **Tier-1 (≤ 0.5 s response):** Brake pedal — the single most important emergency control.
> **Tier-2 (≤ 1.0 s response):** Steering wheel, hazard switch, horn.
> **Tier-3 (≤ 2.5 s response):** Fuel cutoff, E-brake, seatbelt pretensioner trigger.

#### C. By Feedback Modality
* **Visual** — Red pictogram (ISO 2575: ⚠ for hazard).
* **Auditory** — Horn (110–118 dB at 2 m), hazard flasher "tick" (≈ 1.5 Hz).
* **Tactile/Haptic** — Brake pedal "feel" through booster, detent clicks.

### 2.2 Anthropometric Reach Zones (SAE J1100 / J1139)

The driver is referenced from the **Seating Reference Point (SRP)** — the H-point of the SAE J826 manikin when the seat is in its rearmost, lowest design position. Reach zones are spherical shells around the SRP.

| Zone | Name | Reach Radius from SRP | Maximum Operation Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary | Touch zone (P95 reach) | **≤ 600 mm** | < 1 s (ideal) |
| Secondary | Normal reach | **600–1000 mm** | 1–2.5 s |
| Tertiary | Maximum reach (P5 reach) | **1000–1300 mm** | > 2.5 s — NOT for emergency |
| Out-of-zone | Beyond P5 female reach | **> 1300 mm** | Unacceptable for emergency |

### 2.3 Force Capability Limits (95th–5th Percentile Range)

| Control | Body Part | 5th Percentile Female | 95th Percentile Male | KTU Design Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brake Pedal | Foot | **≈ 220 N** | **≈ 680 N** | **≤ 500 N** |
| Steering Wheel (static) | Hand/Arm | **≈ 90 N·m** | **≈ 340 N·m** | **≤ 250 N·m** |
| Hazard Switch Push | Thumb | **≈ 9 N** | **≈ 45 N** | **≤ 20 N** |
| Horn Pad | Palm | **≈ 22 N** | **≈ 90 N** | **≤ 40 N** |
| Door Release | Hand | **≈ 35 N** | **≈ 110 N** | **≤ 70 N** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The design must accommodate the **5th-percentile female (the weakest user)**, NOT the strongest, otherwise the system fails exactly when it is needed most.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Emergency Control Reach & Stopping

$$
\text{Reach}_{\text{design}} \;=\; \mu_{\text{reach}} \;-\; k \cdot \sigma_{\text{reach}}
$$

$$
\text{Stopping Distance} \;=\; v_0 \, t_r \;+\; \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

$$
\text{Perception–Reaction Time } t_r \;=\; t_{\text{perception}} + t_{\text{recognition}} + t_{\text{actuation}}
$$

$$
\text{Pedal Force Margin} \;=\; F_{5\%\,f} \;-\; F_{\text{design}}
$$

$$
\text{RMS Reach Error} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(R_i - R_{\text{nominal}}\right)^{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule (No `|` pipe inside LaTeX):** In any markdown table, use `\vert` for absolute value, e.g. $\vert F_{5\%} - F_{\text{design}} \vert$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Emergency-control accessibility is the *single largest contributor* to active-safety system effectiveness. **Euro NCAP** and **Global NCAP** explicitly score vehicles on the **ease-of-actuation** of hazard controls and the **maximum brake-pedal force** achievable by the smallest driver. A study by the **Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS)** showed that vehicles whose hazard switch was relocated *out of the SAE primary reach zone* had a **22 % higher crash-avoidance failure rate**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reach Envelope Radius for a Target Population

**Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** A vehicle is to be designed to accommodate Indian drivers from the 5th-percentile female (stature 1480 mm) to the 95th-percentile male (stature 1810 mm). If the mean forward functional reach from SRP is **640 mm** with a standard deviation of **42 mm**, determine the design reach envelope radius for the **shortest female** and the **tallest male** (assuming normality, $k = 1.645$ for 95 % one-tail confidence).

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(P)} \;=\; \mu_R \;-\; k \,\sigma_R \quad \text{(for 5th percentile)}
$$

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(P)} \;=\; \mu_R \;+\; k \,\sigma_R \quad \text{(for 95th percentile)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values for the 5th-percentile female.**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(5)} \;=\; 640 \;-\; 1.645 \times 42
$$

**Step 3 — Perform the multiplication explicitly.**

$$
1.645 \times 42 \;=\; 69.09 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract to obtain the lower-bound reach.**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(5)} \;=\; 640 \;-\; 69.09 \;=\; 570.91 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the numerical values for the 95th-percentile male.**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(95)} \;=\; 640 \;+\; 1.645 \times 42
$$

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(95)} \;=\; 640 \;+\; 69.09 \;=\; 709.09 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Decision.**

The hazard switch must be located within **570.91 mm to 709.09 mm** from the SRP to be reachable by the smallest female and not uncomfortably close for the largest male.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Any emergency control placed **> 710 mm** from SRP violates the 95th-percentile male's comfortable reach — but more critically, any control placed **> 570 mm** violates the 5th-percentile female's reach *entirely*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Brake-Pedal Reaction Stopping Distance

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle travelling at **80 km/h** requires the driver to perceive a hazard, react, and apply the brake. Given:

* Perception–Reaction Time $t_r = 1.5$ s
* Maximum achievable deceleration $a_{\max} = 7.5 \;\text{m/s}^2$
* $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$

Compute the total stopping distance.

**Step 1 — Convert speed from km/h to m/s.**

$$
v_0 \;=\; 80 \;\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} \;\times\; \frac{1000 \;\text{m}}{3600 \;\text{s}} \;=\; \frac{80\,000}{3600} \;=\; 22.22 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the standard stopping-distance formula.**

$$
d_{\text{stop}} \;=\; v_0 \, t_r \;+\; \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 \, a_{\max}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the reaction-distance term.**

$$
d_{\text{reaction}} \;=\; 22.22 \times 1.5 \;=\; 33.33 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the braking-distance term.**

$$
d_{\text{brake}} \;=\; \frac{(22.22)^{2}}{2 \times 7.5} \;=\; \frac{493.83}{15} \;=\; 32.92 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum both terms to get the total stopping distance.**

$$
d_{\text{stop}} \;=\; 33.33 \;+\; 32.92 \;=\; 66.25 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation in terms of Emergency Control Accessibility.**

Since the **reaction leg** (33.33 m) depends *entirely* on how fast the driver can actuate the brake, **any ergonomic penalty** (e.g., a brake pedal located outside the primary reach zone, or requiring > 500 N) translates directly into additional metres travelled before deceleration begins.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pedal-Force Margin Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Compute the design margin for a brake pedal if the 5th-percentile female can exert **220 N** and the design specification calls for a maximum actuation force of **180 N**.

**Step 1 — Define the margin.**

$$
\text{Margin} \;=\; F_{5\%\,f} \;-\; F_{\text{design}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{Margin} \;=\; 220 \;-\; 180 \;=\; 40 \;\text{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to a percentage margin relative to capability.**

$$
\%\text{Margin} \;=\; \frac{40}{220} \times 100 \;=\; 18.18\%
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.**

A positive margin of **40 N** means the weakest user has an 18 % reserve above the required force — the design is **ergonomically acceptable** with a recommended safety buffer of ≥ **15 %** as per ISO 9355-3.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Modes of Emergency Controls

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Detection | KTU Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Out-of-reach control | Anthropometric mismatch | Mock-up with P5 dummy | Move into 600 mm radius |
| Excessive pedal force | Booster undersizing | Pedal force rig test | Increase booster ratio |
| Visual confusion | Non-standard pictogram | ISO 2575 audit | Standardize red ⚠ symbol |
| Tactile ambiguity | Soft uniform button | Blindfold test | Add detent / click |
| Acoustic masking | Cabin NVH > horn | Sound-level map | Increase horn SPL to 110+ dB |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Emergency Control Subsystem

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the *flow of driver intent* through the emergency-control subsystem, identifying **actuators**, **transmission paths**, **feedback channels**, and **verification standards**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[driverPanicState] --> B[visualScanOfCockpit]
    B --> C{emergencyControlIdentification}
    C --> D1[hazardSwitchTier2]
    C --> D2[brakePedalTier1]
    C --> D3[hornPadTier2]
    C --> D4[steeringWheelTier2]
    D1 --> E1[electricalActuation12VRelay]
    D2 --> E2[hydraulicBrakeBooster7_5mps2]
    D3 --> E3[hornRelay110dBSPL]
    D4 --> E4[epsTorqueAssist250Nm]
    E1 --> F1[visualFeedbackRedTriangle1_5Hz]
    E2 --> F2[tactileFeedbackPedalFeel]
    E3 --> F3[auditoryFeedback110dB]
    E4 --> F4[hapticFeedbackSteeringTorque]
    F1 --> G[iso2575ComplianceCheck]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[saeJ1139ReachAuditPass]
    H --> I[emergencyControlAcceptance]
```

### 4.2 Reach-Zone Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SRP SeatingReferencePoint] --> B[primaryZone_600mm]
    A --> C[secondaryZone_1000mm]
    A --> D[tertiaryZone_1300mm]
    B --> B1[brakePedal]
    B --> B2[hazardSwitch]
    B --> B3[hornPad]
    C --> C1[gearLever]
    C --> C2[wiperStalk]
    D --> D1[rearDefogger]
    D --> D2[gloveBoxRelease]
    D2 --> X[NOTforEmergency]
    B1 --> Y[approvedEmergency]
    B2 --> Y
    B3 --> Y
```

### 4.3 Force-Compatibility Decision Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[controlForceSpecification] --> B{percentileCheck}
    B --> C[F5femaleGreaterThanDesign]
    B --> D[F95maleLessThanDesign]
    C --> E[marginCheck_positive]
    D --> F[marginCheck_positive]
    E --> G[designAcceptable]
    F --> G
    C --> H[F5femaleLessThanDesign]
    H --> I[designReject_redesignRequired]
    D --> J[F95maleGreaterThanDesign]
    J --> K[designReject_forceTooHigh]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for diagrams:** Each block follows the convention `nodeIdentifier_CapitalizedLabel`. The branch `X[NOTforEmergency]` at the bottom-right of Section 4.2 indicates that any control placed in the tertiary (>1000 mm) reach zone is **automatically disqualified** from being classified as an emergency control under KTU 2024 evaluation rubrics.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define "Emergency Controls" in the context of automotive ergonomics. List any four examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Emergency controls are driver–vehicle interface elements designed to be **located, identified, and actuated within 2.5–3.0 seconds** under panic or time-critical conditions, in order to prevent or mitigate an accident.

Four examples (1 mark for the definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for the list):

1. Brake pedal.
2. Hazard warning switch.
3. Horn.
4. Steering wheel (for evasive manoeuvres).
5. *(Optional fifth: fuel-cutoff switch, seatbelt pretensioner trigger.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the "Three-Second Rule" with respect to emergency-control accessibility.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Three-Second Rule (1 mark) states that a properly restrained, seatbelted driver must be able to **locate** the emergency control within **1.0 s**, **identify** it within **0.5 s**, and **actuate** it within **1.0 s** (1 mark), giving a **total of ≤ 3.0 seconds** from perception to action (1 mark). This rule ensures that the control remains useful under panic conditions and is used as the design target in SAE J1139 reach-envelope audits.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Part (a): 7 Marks** *(Choice 1)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the three reach zones (primary, secondary, tertiary) used in the design of passenger-car emergency controls. Quote the relevant SAE/ISO standard.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Reach zones are spherical shells defined around the Seating Reference Point (SRP)** per **SAE J1100 and SAE J1139**.

* **Primary reach zone** (2 marks) — Radius **≤ 600 mm** from the SRP. All **Tier-1** emergency controls (brake pedal, hazard switch, horn pad, steering wheel) must lie within this zone. Operating time < 1 s; accessible to the 5th-percentile female without torso lean.
* **Secondary reach zone** (2 marks) — Radius **600–1000 mm**. Acceptable for **Tier-3** controls (gear lever, wiper stalk). Operating time 1–2.5 s; may require a slight torso lean.
* **Tertiary reach zone** (2 marks) — Radius **1000–1300 mm**. Reserved for non-emergency controls (glove-box release, rear defogger). Operating time > 2.5 s — explicitly **disqualified** from being an emergency control.
* **Standard reference** (1 mark): **SAE J1139 — "Driver-Front Seat Reach envelopes"**, complemented by **ISO 3958** for hand-control placement.

---

#### **Question A — Part (b): 7 Marks**

*A vehicle is designed such that the brake pedal is located 720 mm from the SRP. The mean forward reach from the SRP for the target population is 640 mm with a standard deviation of 42 mm. Using $k = 1.645$ (95 % one-tail), determine:*
*(i) the reach envelope for the 5th-percentile female.*
*(ii) whether the brake-pedal location is accessible to her.*
*(iii) the recommended redesign distance.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the governing equation (1 mark).**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(5)} \;=\; \mu_R \;-\; k \,\sigma_R
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values and compute (2 marks).**

$$
R_{\text{design}}^{(5)} \;=\; 640 \;-\; 1.645 \times 42 \;=\; 640 \;-\; 69.09 \;=\; 570.91 \;\text{mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to brake-pedal location (1 mark).**

The pedal is at **720 mm**, but the 5th-percentile female can reach only **570.91 mm**.

$$
\Delta \;=\; 720 \;-\; 570.91 \;=\; 149.09 \;\text{mm} \quad \text{short}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark).** The pedal is **inaccessible** to the 5th-percentile female — the design fails SAE J1139.

**Step 5 — Recommended redesign (2 marks).** Move the brake pedal **closer to the SRP by at least 150 mm**, i.e., to a target location of **≤ 570 mm** from the SRP, ideally **540–560 mm** to allow a 30 mm safety buffer.

---

#### **Question B — Part (a): 7 Marks** *(Choice 2)*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Explain the concept of "Force Compatibility" in emergency-control design. Tabulate the maximum permissible actuation force for brake pedal, steering wheel, hazard switch, and horn pad.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Force compatibility is the principle that the actuation force required by an emergency control must lie **below the capability of the weakest intended user** (the 5th-percentile female) and **above the inadvertent-actuation threshold** for the strongest (95th-percentile male) — a *Goldilocks zone* of force.

**Tabulation (4 marks):**

| Control | Body Part | KTU Design Limit |
|---|---|---|
| Brake Pedal | Foot | **≤ 500 N** |
| Steering Wheel (static) | Hand/Arm | **≤ 250 N·m** |
| Hazard Switch Push | Thumb | **≤ 20 N** |
| Horn Pad | Palm | **≤ 40 N** |

**Engineering interpretation (1 mark):** A brake pedal requiring > 500 N fails because an average female cannot produce that force repeatedly in a panic stop; a horn pad requiring < 10 N fails because it would beep on every bump.

---

#### **Question B — Part (b): 7 Marks**

*A driver perceives a hazard at 80 km/h. The perception–reaction time is 1.5 s and the maximum achievable deceleration is 7.5 m/s². Compute the total stopping distance and the fraction attributable to the perception–reaction leg.*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert speed (1 mark).**

$$
v_0 \;=\; 80 \times \tfrac{1000}{3600} \;=\; 22.22 \;\text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Reaction distance (2 marks).**

$$
d_{\text{reaction}} \;=\; v_0 \, t_r \;=\; 22.22 \times 1.5 \;=\; 33.33 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 3 — Braking distance (2 marks).**

$$
d_{\text{brake}} \;=\; \frac{v_0^{2}}{2 a_{\max}} \;=\; \frac{(22.22)^{2}}{2 \times 7.5} \;=\; 32.92 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 4 — Total stopping distance (1 mark).**

$$
d_{\text{stop}} \;=\; 33.33 \;+\; 32.92 \;=\; 66.25 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Reaction fraction (1 mark).**

$$
f_{\text{reaction}} \;=\; \frac{33.33}{66.25} \times 100 \;=\; 50.31 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** **Over 50 %** of the stopping distance is spent *before* the brake is even applied — proving that ergonomic accessibility of the brake pedal is **as critical as brake hardware itself**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Rubric):**
> 1. **Confusing reach zones with seating posture zones** — students frequently list "forward / mid / rear" instead of "primary / secondary / tertiary." The KTU key explicitly demands the *radius in mm* alongside the zone name.
> 2. **Using the 95th-percentile as the weakest user** — a classic reverse-logic error. The *5th-percentile female* defines the *minimum capability*; design *must accommodate her*, not the average.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** in stopping-distance problems — a one-step error that costs **2 full marks** in Part B numericals.
> 4. **Omitting the standard reference** (SAE J1139 / ISO 3958 / ISO 2575). Each standard citation in a Part B answer carries **½ to 1 bonus mark** under KTU 2024 evaluation.
> 5. **Using `|` for absolute value inside a markdown table** — breaks the table parser in the digital valuation portal; always use `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Topic: Emergency Controls and Their Accessibility**
> - Emergency controls = **time-critical HMIs**, designed under the **Three-Second Rule** (locate 1 s + identify 0.5 s + actuate 1 s).
> - Three primary emergency controls: **brake pedal, hazard switch, horn**.
> - Reach zones (from SRP): **Primary ≤ 600 mm**, **Secondary 600–1000 mm**, **Tertiary 1000–1300 mm**.
> - **Only Primary-zone controls** qualify as emergency controls under KTU 2024.
> - Design must accommodate the **5th-percentile female** (the weakest user).
> - Standard brake-pedal force limit: **≤ 500 N**; steering static torque: **≤ 250 N·m**; hazard switch: **≤ 20 N**; horn pad: **≤ 40 N**.
> - Reach formula: $R = \mu \pm k\sigma$ with **$k = 1.645$** for 95 % one-tail confidence.
> - Stopping distance: $d = v_0 t_r + v_0^{2}/(2a_{\max})$; perception–reaction leg ≈ **50 %** of total at highway speeds.
> - Colour code: **red pictogram ⚠** for hazard per **ISO 2575**.
> - Reach-envelope audit standards: **SAE J1100** (H-point), **SAE J1139** (reach envelopes), **ISO 3958** (hand controls).
> - Force-compatibility "Goldilocks zone" principle: above *inadvertent-actuation* threshold, below *5th-percentile capability*.
> - Visual + auditory + tactile feedback must be **redundant** (at least two modalities per control).
> - Always convert **km/h → m/s** by multiplying by **1000/3600 = 5/18** in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ergonomics in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ergonomics in Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** are electronic systems embedded within a vehicle that use advanced sensors (cameras, radar, LiDAR, ultrasonic), sophisticated algorithms, and Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI) to assist the driver in the dynamic driving task. According to the **SAE International Standard J3016**, ADAS spans **Levels 0 through 2** of driving automation, where the *human driver* retains primary control, but receives continuous or event-based assistance.

**Ergonomics in ADAS** is the scientific discipline concerned with optimizing the *interaction* between the driver, the machine (the vehicle's intelligent subsystems), and the operational environment. It focuses on the design of displays, controls, warning strategies, sensor placement (anthropometric fit), and information-flow architecture to ensure **safety, situational awareness, trust calibration, and minimized cognitive load** during the Human-Machine Cooperation loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Ergonomics in ADAS specifically bridges *occupant ergonomics* (Module 2) with *active safety systems*. The examiner expects the student to know that ADAS ergonomics is **not just about displays** — it is about the **complete cooperative driver-vehicle partnership** governed by feedback, trust, and workload.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Co-Pilot" Metaphor

Imagine you are flying a small two-seater aircraft. You are the *Pilot-in-Command*, but seated next to you is a **Co-Pilot** (the ADAS). The Co-Pilot does *not* take over your job entirely — instead, the Co-Pilot:
- **Watches the instruments** so your eyes don't have to (Adaptive Cruise Control).
- **Shouts "Pull Up!"** when terrain gets too close (Forward Collision Warning).
- **Nudges the yoke** gently when the aircraft drifts (Lane Keeping Assist).
- **Watches whether YOU are paying attention** (Driver Monitoring System).

The entire flight is *safe* only if:
1. The Co-Pilot's voice is **clearly audible** (auditory ergonomics).
2. The Co-Pilot's instrument panel is **glance-readable** in 1.5 seconds (visual ergonomics).
3. You, the pilot, **trust the Co-Pilot** appropriately (trust calibration).
4. You don't become so comfortable that you **fall asleep** (automation complacency).

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy maps directly onto the engineering reality: ADAS is a **Cooperative System**, and *ergonomics* ensures the cooperation remains stable, safe, and intuitive.

### 1.3 Core Metrics and Standardized Terminology

| Standard / Metric | Value / Definition | Relevance to Ergonomics |
|---|---|---|
| **SAE J3016 Level 0** | No Automation | Baseline — pure human performance |
| **SAE J3016 Level 1** | Driver Assistance (single function, e.g., ACC) | Single-task handover |
| **SAE J3016 Level 2** | Partial Automation (combined, e.g., ACC + LCC) | Sustained shared control |
| **NHTSA Eyes-Off-Road Time Limit** | **2 seconds** (recommended glance) | Display design constraint |
| **Mean Reaction Time (brake)** | **1.3 s** (alert driver) | Warning timing constraint |
| **Field of View (legal driving)** | **≈ 180°** | Sensor placement |
| **Preferred HUD Refresh Rate** | **≥ 60 Hz** | Avoid flicker-induced discomfort |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Taxonomy of ADAS — Operational Logic

ADAS can be hierarchically classified by the *driving function* it supports. The taxonomy is critical for ergonomic reasoning because each function demands a different **HMI strategy**.

**A. Longitudinal Control (Accel/Brake):**
- **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control):** Maintains time-headway to a forward vehicle.
- **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking):** Detects imminent collision and applies brakes.
- **Ergonomic Concern:** The driver must understand *who* is braking and *why*. Mode confusion arises if the transition between manual and automated braking is ambiguous.

**B. Lateral Control (Steering):**
- **LKA (Lane Keeping Assist):** Applies corrective steering torque.
- **LCA (Lane Centering Assist):** Continuously steers to maintain lane-center.
- **Ergonomic Concern:** Haptic feedback through the steering wheel must be *informative*, not *alarming*. Excessive torque (a "nagging" wheel) causes user disablement.

**C. Perception & Warning:**
- **FCW (Forward Collision Warning), LDW (Lane Departure Warning), BSW (Blind Spot Warning).**
- **Ergonomic Concern:** Warning *timing*, *modality* (visual/auditory/haptic), and *intensity* must follow the **ISO 15005** and **ISO 9241** standards.

**D. Parking & Maneuvering:**
- **Park Assist, Rear Cross Traffic Alert, 360° Surround View.**
- **Ergonomic Concern:** Display layout must be **anthropometrically reachable** and **glanceable** during low-speed maneuvers.

**E. Driver Monitoring:**
- **DMS (Driver Monitoring System):** Tracks gaze, head pose, eyelid closure (PERCLOS).
- **Ergonomic Concern:** Sensor placement must respect the **driver's anthropometric range** (5th–95th percentile) without occluding the road view.

### 2.2 Why Ergonomics in ADAS is *Non-Trivial*

The core engineering "Why" behind ADAS ergonomics rests on three pillars:

1. **The Automation Paradox (Parasuraman & Riley, 1997):** The better the automation, the worse the human's vigilance. Ergonomic design must counteract complacency.
2. **Trust Calibration:** If drivers *over-trust* ADAS, they disengage; if they *under-trust*, they disable it. The HMI must convey *system competence* honestly.
3. **Mode Awareness:** Drivers must always know *which* ADAS function is active. Poor mode awareness causes the infamous "mode confusion" accidents (e.g., Tesla Autopilot disengagement scenarios).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the *mathematical backbone* of ADAS ergonomics. Master these for Part A numerical and Part B derivations.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definition | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | $\Delta d$: relative distance (m); $\Delta v$: relative velocity (m/s) | Core metric for FCW/AEB warning timing |
| 2 | **Time-Headway (THW)** | $THW = \dfrac{d_{gap}}{v_{ego}}$ | $d_{gap}$: bumper-to-bumper gap; $v_{ego}$: ego speed | Sets ACC following distance |
| 3 | **Brake Reaction Time (Total Stopping)** | $d_{stop} = v \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}$ | $v$: speed; $t_{react}$: reaction time; $\mu$: friction coeff.; $g$: **9.81 m/s²** | Defines safe FCW trigger threshold |
| 4 | **Kinematic Minimum Risk Maneuver Acceleration** | $a_{brake} = \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 \cdot d_{available}}$ | $a_{brake}$: required decel; $d_{available}$: free space | For AEB braking profile |
| 5 | **PERCLOS (Drowsiness Index)** | $PERCLOS = \dfrac{t_{closed}}{t_{window}}$ | $t_{closed}$: eyelid closure time; $t_{window}$: observation window (e.g., 1 min) | DMS fatigue detection threshold ≈ **0.15** |
| 6 | **Workload Index (NASA-TLX Weighted)** | $TLX = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_{i} \cdot r_{i}$ | $w_{i}$: weight of sub-scale; $r_{i}$: raw score (0–100) | Subjective ADAS load evaluation |
| 7 | **Detection Accuracy (Signal Detection Theory)** | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | $z(H)$: z-score of Hit rate; $z(F)$: z-score of False alarm | HMI warning effectiveness metric |
| 8 | **Optical Hud Virtual Image Distance** | $D_{virtual} = f \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{M}\right)$ | $f$: focal length; $M$: magnification | HUD ergonomics design |
| 9 | **Steering Torque for LKA Haptic** | $\tau_{LKA} = k_{p} \cdot e_{lat} + k_{d} \cdot \dot{e}_{lat}$ | $e_{lat}$: lateral error; $k_{p}, k_{d}$: PD gains | Tuning for "informative" steering feel |
| 10 | **Driver Availability (DMS)** | $A = f(\text{Gaze}, \text{PERCLOS}, \text{Head Pose})$ | Composite metric | Triggers Takeover Request (TOR) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Toolchain:** These formulas drive embedded C-code in ECU units (Bosch, Continental, Aptiv).
- **Type Approval:** EU GSR (General Safety Regulation) mandates AEB, LDW, ELKS based on derived TTC thresholds.
- **Insurance & NCAP:** Euro NCAP 5-star rating explicitly scores **AEB and Lane Support** ergonomic effectiveness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-To-Collision (TTC) and the FCW Trigger Threshold

The **TTC** is the time remaining before two vehicles collide, *assuming* current speed and trajectory are maintained. It is the *single most important* kinematic variable in ADAS ergonomics because it dictates when a warning must fire.

**Step 1 — Define the kinematic state.**

Let the ego vehicle travel at velocity $v_{e}$ and the lead vehicle travel at velocity $v_{l}$, separated by a bumper-to-bumper distance $d$.

The relative velocity is:
$$\Delta v = v_{e} - v_{l}$$

**Step 2 — Define relative distance.**

The relative distance is the *current* gap:
$$\Delta d = d$$

**Step 3 — Compute Time-To-Collision.**

By definition, TTC is the time for the relative gap to close to zero at the *constant* relative rate $\Delta v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At time } t &: \quad \Delta d(t) = \Delta d_{0} - \Delta v \cdot t \\
\text{Collision condition} &: \quad \Delta d(t_{c}) = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \quad 0 &= \Delta d_{0} - \Delta v \cdot t_{c} \\
\Rightarrow \quad t_{c} &= \dfrac{\Delta d_{0}}{\Delta v}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, by definition:
$$TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the simplest *steady-state* TTC. For curved roads, a *predicted* TTC using future-path intersection must be used, but the fundamental ratio form remains.

**Step 4 — Apply to a numerical KTU-style problem.**

*Ego vehicle:* $v_{e} = 30 \text{ m/s}$ (~108 km/h). *Lead vehicle:* $v_{l} = 20 \text{ m/s}$ (~72 km/h). *Gap:* $\Delta d = 25 \text{ m}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta v &= v_{e} - v_{l} = 30 - 20 = 10 \text{ m/s} \\
TTC &= \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{25 \text{ m}}{10 \text{ m/s}} = 2.5 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Determine the FCW ergonomic trigger.**

A standard ergonomic *warning* should fire at $TTC \le 2.5 \text{ s}$ (auditory + visual), while *automatic brake pre-charge* activates at $TTC \le 1.5 \text{ s}$, and *full AEB* at $TTC \le 0.8 \text{ s}$. These thresholds are tuned via **human-in-the-loop simulator studies**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Stopping Distance and AEB Ergonomic Window

For an AEB system to be effective, the warning *and* automatic brake must fire in time for the vehicle to stop *within* the available distance.

**Step 1 — Decompose stopping distance.**

Total stopping distance = **Perception-Reaction Distance** + **Braking Distance**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{perception} &= v_{ego} \cdot t_{react} \\
d_{braking} &= \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 \mu g} \\
d_{total} &= v_{ego} \cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{ego}^{2}}{2 \mu g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply engineering values.**

Let $v_{ego} = 25 \text{ m/s}$ (90 km/h), $t_{react} = 1.3 \text{ s}$, $\mu = 0.7$ (dry asphalt), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{perception} &= 25 \times 1.3 = 32.5 \text{ m} \\
d_{braking} &= \dfrac{25^{2}}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{625}{13.734} \approx 45.5 \text{ m} \\
d_{total} &= 32.5 + 45.5 = 78.0 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ergonomic interpretation.**

If the obstacle is detected at *exactly* 78 m, AEB must act at the *moment of detection* — there is no human-reaction buffer. The HMI's *job* is to:
- Display the distance continuously (visual, glanceable in 1 s).
- Provide a graduated haptic seat vibration at $d = 60 \text{ m}$ (perceptual cue).
- Issue an auditory "BRAKE" at $d = 45 \text{ m}$.

This *layered* warning strategy follows the **Multiple Resource Theory** (Wickens, 2008) — distributing alerts across modalities to avoid sensory-channel overload.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: PERCLOS Drowsiness Index

The **PERCLOS** index measures the proportion of time the driver's eyelids are *closed* (typically $\ge 80\%$) over a sliding observation window. It is the *gold-standard* DMS metric.

**Step 1 — Define measurement window.**

A 60-second observation window is used by most OEMs (e.g., Seeing Machines, Smart Eye).

$$t_{window} = 60 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Measure closure time.**

The vision-based system sums all frames (or sub-windows) where the eye is $\ge 80\%$ closed. If 9 seconds in 60 s show closure, then:

$$t_{closed} = 9 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute PERCLOS.**

$$
PERCLOS = \dfrac{t_{closed}}{t_{window}} = \dfrac{9}{60} = 0.15
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic thresholding.**

| PERCLOS Range | Driver State | System Action |
|---|---|---|
| $< 0.08$ | Alert | No action |
| $0.08 \le P < 0.15$ | Slight drowsiness | Visual coffee-cup icon |
| $0.15 \le P < 0.25$ | Drowsy | Audible chime + haptic seat |
| $\ge 0.25$ | Severely drowsy | Escalating warning; suggest rest stop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The threshold of **0.15** is a *consensus* value derived from large-scale naturalistic driving studies (e.g., SHRP 2, U.S. DOT). In KTU answers, always cite the *trigger threshold* and the *modality of warning* together — examiners reward *systemic* thinking.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: HMI Modality Selection (Wickens' Model)

| Driving Sub-task | Primary Modality | Why? | Pitfall if Mismatched |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward obstacle warning | **Auditory** (3D spatial) | Visual channel is road-occupied | Driver misses warning |
| Lane drift | **Haptic** (steering torque) | Hands are on the wheel | Distracts if visual |
| Speed limit change | **Visual HUD** | Information density | Auditory overloads speech channel |
| Drowsiness | **Haptic seat + Auditory** | Eyes are closing | Visual is useless |
| Navigation turn | **Auditory (speech)** | Eyes-on-road | Visual clutter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SAE J3016 Levels of Driving Automation — Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the *cooperation boundary* between human and machine at each ADAS level. This is the single most important figure a KTU examiner expects you to reproduce.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0["LEVEL 0: NO AUTOMATION\nHuman drives; ADAS issues warnings only\nExample: LDW, FCW, BSW"]
    L1["LEVEL 1: DRIVER ASSISTANCE\nOne function automated (longitudinal OR lateral)\nExample: ACC alone OR LCC alone"]
    L2["LEVEL 2: PARTIAL AUTOMATION\nLongitudinal AND lateral combined\nExample: Tesla Autopilot basic, GM Super Cruise"]
    L3["LEVEL 3: CONDITIONAL AUTOMATION\nVehicle drives; human is fallback\nExample: Mercedes DRIVE PILOT (Germany)"]
    L4["LEVEL 4: HIGH AUTOMATION\nGeofenced self-driving; no human needed\nExample: Robotaxi pilot zones"]
    L5["LEVEL 5: FULL AUTOMATION\nNo steering wheel; any condition"]

    L0 --> L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    HCOOP["HUMAN-MACHINE COOPERATION LOOP\nDriver monitors system\nSystem monitors driver (DMS)"] -.-> L1
    HCOOP -.-> L2
    HCOOP -.-> L3

    TRUST["TRUST CALIBRATION\nRequired at every level\nHMI must communicate capability honestly"] -.-> L1
    TRUST -.-> L2
    TRUST -.-> L3
```

### 4.2 Driver-Vehicle Cooperation Loop (HMI Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the *information flow* that ergonomics must optimize.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENV["ROAD ENVIRONMENT\nRoad signs, vehicles, pedestrians, weather"]
    SENS["VEHICLE SENSORS\nCamera, Radar, LiDAR, Ultrasonic, GPS"]
    PROC["ADAS PROCESSOR\nSensor fusion + decision algorithm"]
    ACT["VEHICLE ACTUATORS\nBrake, Steer, Throttle, HMI Displays"]
    DRV["HUMAN DRIVER\nPerception, Cognition, Motor response"]
    DMS["DRIVER MONITORING SYSTEM\nGaze, PERCLOS, Head pose"]
    HMI["HMI OUTPUTS\nVisual HUD, Center stack, Auditory, Haptic seat"]
    TURN["TAKEOVER REQUEST TOR\nMulti-modal escalation"]

    ENV --> SENS
    SENS --> PROC
    PROC --> ACT
    PROC --> HMI
    HMI --> DRV
    DRV --> ACT
    ACT --> ENV
    DRV --> DMS
    DMS --> PROC
    PROC --> TURN
    TURN --> DRV
```

### 4.3 ADAS Warning Escalation Hierarchy (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DETECT["SENSOR DETECTION\nObject classified\nTTC computed"]
    L1W["TIER 1: ADVISORY\nIcon on HUD\nThreshold TTC 3.0 s"]
    L2W["TIER 2: CAUTION\nAudible chime + haptic seat\nThreshold TTC 2.0 s"]
    L3W["TIER 3: WARNING\nRepeated beep + brake pre-charge\nThreshold TTC 1.2 s"]
    L4W["TIER 4: INTERVENTION\nFull AEB activated\nThreshold TTC 0.6 s"]
    POST["POST-EVENT\nDriver feedback, log upload"]

    DETECT --> L1W
    L1W --> L2W
    L2W --> L3W
    L3W --> L4W
    L4W --> POST
```

### 4.4 Driver Monitoring System (DMS) Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IRLED["IR LED ILLUMINATOR\n850 nm or 940 nm wavelength"]
    EYE["DRIVER FACE AND EYE REGION\nAcquired via IR camera"]
    ALG["VISION ALGORITHM\nCNN + landmark detection"]
    GAZE["GAZE VECTOR ESTIMATION\n3D gaze direction"]
    HEAD["HEAD POSE ESTIMATION\nYaw, pitch, roll angles"]
    PRC["PERCLOS COMPUTATION\nSliding window 60 s"]
    TRN["TURN SIGNAL / BLINKER MONITORING\nLane change intent"]
    DEC["FUSION DECISION LOGIC\nAlertness state classification"]
    ACT["HMI ESCALATION\nCoffee icon, chime, seat vibration, TOR"]

    IRLED --> EYE
    EYE --> ALG
    ALG --> GAZE
    ALG --> HEAD
    ALG --> PRC
    GAZE --> DEC
    HEAD --> DEC
    PRC --> DEC
    TRN --> DEC
    DEC --> ACT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define ADAS. List any four ADAS functions and state the primary ergonomic challenge associated with each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — concise but complete):**

ADAS stands for *Advanced Driver Assistance Systems*. They are sensor-driven electronic subsystems that assist the human driver in longitudinal control, lateral control, perception, and parking.

| # | ADAS Function | Primary Ergonomic Challenge |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) | Driver must understand *automated vs. manual* braking — *mode awareness*. |
| 2 | Lane Keeping Assist (LKA) | Steering torque must be *informative* without being *alarming* (avoid user disablement). |
| 3 | Forward Collision Warning (FCW) | Warning *modality* and *timing* must match driver reaction time (~1.3 s). |
| 4 | Driver Monitoring System (DMS) | IR sensor placement must respect the **5th–95th percentile** driver anthropometric range. |

*(Examiner will award 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the four functions with ergonomic challenges — ½ mark each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Equivalent]**
*State the SAE J3016 levels of driving automation. Why is Level 2 considered the most ergonomically critical level for human-machine cooperation?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SAE J3016 Levels:**
- **Level 0** — No Automation
- **Level 1** — Driver Assistance (single function)
- **Level 2** — Partial Automation (combined longitudinal + lateral)
- **Level 3** — Conditional Automation
- **Level 4** — High Automation
- **Level 5** — Full Automation

**Why Level 2 is ergonomically critical:** At Level 2, the *human driver* is *legally and operationally responsible* for monitoring the road, even though the vehicle simultaneously controls both longitudinal and lateral motion. This creates the **Human-Supervised Automation Paradox** — the driver must remain vigilant, but the smooth automation induces **complacency**, **mode confusion**, and **delayed takeover**. Hence, the **HMI ergonomics** (warning design, mode indicators, gaze monitoring) is the *primary safety lever* at this level.

*(1 mark for listing 6 levels, 2 marks for the Level 2 explanation.)*

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU ESE Part B has internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the *Human-Machine Cooperation Loop* in ADAS with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Driver Monitoring System (DMS)** in maintaining loop integrity.

**Model Solution:**

The Human-Machine Cooperation Loop in ADAS is a closed-loop control system in which:
- The **vehicle** senses the environment, processes data, and acts on actuators.
- The **human driver** perceives, decides, and operates controls.
- The **HMI** mediates information flow between them.

**Block diagram (verbal description to be drawn on answer sheet):**

> ROAD → SENSORS → ADAS PROCESSOR → ACTUATORS → VEHICLE → ROAD  
> ↑  
> DRIVER (via steering, pedals)  
> ↑  
> HMI (HUD, audio, haptic) ← ADAS PROCESSOR  
> ↑  
> DMS (monitors driver gaze, PERCLOS) → ADAS PROCESSOR (closed loop)

**Role of DMS (Key Points for 7 marks):**

1. **Detects driver drowsiness** using PERCLOS index (threshold **0.15**).
2. **Detects driver distraction** via off-road gaze detection (off-road > 2 s).
3. **Closes the cooperation loop** by feeding driver state back to the ADAS processor.
4. **Enables adaptive HMI** — escalates warnings if driver is *less* attentive.
5. **Provides takeover readiness assessment** for Level 2/3 systems.
6. **Anthropometrically constrained sensor placement** (steering column-top or cluster) ensures coverage of 5th–95th percentile drivers.
7. **Legal & insurance compliance** — increasingly required under EU GSR 2 (2024).

*(Allocate 3 marks for the diagram description and 4 marks for the DMS explanation, with each sub-bullet carrying ~½ mark.)*

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable derivation, explain how the **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** is used as the *primary ergonomic trigger* for Forward Collision Warning (FCW) and Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB). Use $v_{e} = 90$ km/h, $v_{l} = 60$ km/h, gap = 40 m.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units.**
$v_{e} = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s; $v_{l} = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Compute relative velocity.**
$$\Delta v = v_{e} - v_{l} = 25 - 16.67 = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply TTC formula.**
$$TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{40}{8.33} \approx 4.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic interpretation & threshold mapping.**

| TTC (s) | Ergonomic Action |
|---|---|
| $\ge 4.0$ | No action |
| $2.5 \le TTC < 4.0$ | Visual HUD icon (advisory) |
| $1.5 \le TTC < 2.5$ | Audible chime + haptic seat (caution) |
| $0.8 \le TTC < 1.5$ | Brake pre-charge + repeated beep (warning) |
| $< 0.8$ | Full AEB intervention |

Since computed $TTC = 4.8$ s, the system remains silent. As the gap closes, when TTC crosses **2.5 s**, the HUD activates; **1.5 s**, audible chime; **0.8 s**, AEB. This *graduated escalation* is the cornerstone of ergonomic warning design (ISO 15623, ISO 22839).

*(7 marks: 1 mark unit conversion, 1 mark relative velocity, 1 mark TTC computation, 4 marks for ergonomic thresholding table and interpretation.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **HMI design principles** for ADAS. Explain the role of *modality selection* using **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory** in warning design.

**Model Solution:**

**Core HMI Design Principles (4 marks):**

1. **Glanceability:** Information must be readable within **1.5 s** of road glance (NHTSA limit).
2. **Consistency:** Same icon, color, and sound across all ADAS modes.
3. **Salience:** Critical warnings must capture attention without startling (use *red* sparingly — only for imminent danger).
4. **Minimal Cognitive Load:** Avoid sending visual + auditory + haptic alerts simultaneously unless the threat is *imminent*.
5. **Trust Calibration:** HMI must not *over-sell* system capability (e.g., do not name a Level 2 system "Autopilot").
6. **Anthropometric Compatibility:** Displays and touch controls within easy reach envelope of 5th–95th percentile driver.
7. **Internationalization:** Icons over text for cross-language safety (ISO 7010).

**Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (3 marks):**

Wickens (2008) proposed that human information processing consists of *independent* resource pools (visual, auditory, cognitive, manual). The theory predicts that **concurrent tasks using different modalities interfere less** than tasks sharing a modality.

**Application to ADAS warning design:**
- **Visual + Auditory separation:** A driver *looking* at the road can still *hear* a FCW beep.
- **Haptic (seat/steering) for spatial information:** A left-side haptic pulse for left-blind-spot alert uses the *manual/spatial* channel, avoiding visual load.
- **Why mixed-modality helps:** The driver has *more spare capacity* in each channel, reducing reaction time and missed-warning rate.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *over-apply* multi-modal warnings (fire visual + auditory + haptic at the *same* threshold). This causes *sensory overload* and *annoyance*, leading to user disablement. The correct ergonomic design is **graduated escalation** — start visual-only, then add modalities as severity increases. **Marks are lost for monolithic warnings.**

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** A DMS system measures PERCLOS = 0.20 in a 60-second window. (i) Interpret the driver's state. (ii) Design a *graduated ergonomic intervention* strategy for the HMI. (iii) Justify the *anthropometric* placement of the IR camera inside the cabin.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Driver State Interpretation (2 marks):**

Since the threshold for *drowsiness intervention* is **PERCLOS ≥ 0.15**, a value of **0.20** indicates the driver is in the **Drowsy** state, between *caution* (0.15) and *severe* (0.25).

**(ii) Graduated HMI Intervention (3 marks):**

| Tier | HMI Action | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 (already active at P=0.15) | Coffee-cup icon on cluster | Subtle visual nudge |
| Tier 2 (P=0.20, current) | Audible chime **+** haptic seat vibration | Escalate to multi-modal |
| Tier 3 (P=0.25) | Repeated audible + visual "PULL OVER" + suggest nearest rest stop | Pre-failure warning |
| Tier 4 (P=0.35) | Reduce ADAS automation level (degrade L2 to L1) | Reduce complexity; force driver engagement |
| Tier 5 (P>0.40) | Escalate to Takeover Request; activate hazard lights | Failure-prevention mode |

**(iii) Anthropometric Camera Placement (2 marks):**

The IR camera must:
- Be positioned on the **steering column** or **top of instrument cluster**, giving a clear view of the face regardless of seat position.
- Be aimed to cover the **5th-percentile female** (shorter, seat forward) to the **95th-percentile male** (taller, seat rearward) — total reach range ≈ **200 mm** vertical, **150 mm** longitudinal.
- Be mounted at a vertical angle that **does not occlude the forward road view** (typically < 15° from the horizontal).
- Be **fail-safe** — if the camera lens is occluded (e.g., sunlight), the DMS must *gracefully degrade* and the HMI must *notify* the driver.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Loss:** Students often place the DMS camera on the *windshield* (occluding the road) or on the *A-pillar* (losing eye-region coverage). Examiner will *deduct 1 mark* if you do not justify the placement using an *anthropometric* argument.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU 2024 ESE on ADAS Ergonomics.**

- ✅ **ADAS definition** = sensor + algorithm + HMI systems assisting the driver (SAE Levels 0–2, by strict definition).
- ✅ **SAE J3016** has **6 levels** (0 to 5). Level 2 is *the* ergonomics-critical level.
- ✅ **Three pillars** of ADAS ergonomics: (1) Trust calibration, (2) Mode awareness, (3) Workload management.
- ✅ **TTC = Δd / Δv** — the master kinematic trigger variable.
- ✅ **Reaction time = 1.3 s** (alert driver) — drives FCW/AEB thresholds.
- ✅ **PERCLOS threshold = 0.15** for drowsiness intervention (DMS).
- ✅ **NHTSA glance time ≤ 2 s** — defines display glanceability.
- ✅ **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory** justifies multi-modal warning design.
- ✅ **Graduated warning hierarchy:** Advisory → Caution → Warning → Intervention.
- ✅ **Anthropometric design range:** 5th–95th percentile (≈ 200 mm vertical seat travel, 150 mm longitudinal).
- ✅ **Common ADAS HMI outputs:** HUD, center-stack display, instrument cluster, auditory chimes, haptic seat/steering.
- ✅ **Legal frameworks:** EU GSR 2 (2024), ISO 15623 (FCW), ISO 22839 (AEB), ISO 15005 (in-vehicle HMI).
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Confusing *warning* with *intervention* — they have *different* TTC thresholds and *different* ergonomic roles.
- ✅ **Remember the *Co-Pilot analogy*** — use it whenever a question asks for a "real-world" description.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User-Friendly Interfaces for ADAS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** User-Friendly Interfaces for ADAS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **User-Friendly Interface for Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** is a *Human-Machine Interface (HMI)* that mediates information transfer between the vehicle's automated perception, planning, and actuation subsystems and the human driver, while respecting the ergonomic constraints of human cognition, perception, and motor response. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this interface is engineered to:

- Convey vehicle state, environmental hazards, and intervention requests without exceeding the driver's available **attentional bandwidth** (typically **1.0–1.5 channels** of Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory).
- Trigger **calibrated trust** in automation (neither over-reliance nor disuse).
- Comply with international standards such as **ISO 15005** (dialogue management), **ISO 15006** (auditory presentation), **ISO 16673** (occlusion method for visual distraction), and **NHTSA Driver Distraction Guidelines (FMVSS 101-E)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box — ADAS HMI**
> An ADAS HMI is the integrated cluster of visual displays, auditory alerts, haptic feedback, and multimodal interaction modalities through which the driver negotiates **Levels 0–5 of driving automation** (SAE J3016). At SAE L2 and L3, the HMI is *safety-critical*; at L4 and L5, it becomes *comfort-critical*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Co-Pilot's Whisper**
> Imagine a thoughtful co-pilot sitting next to you while you fly a small aircraft. The co-pilot *never grabs the yoke* unless you are about to crash. Instead, they:
> 1. Whisper warnings ("Mountain ahead, pull up") in good time.
> 2. Lightly nudge the control column (**haptic cue**) when you drift.
> 3. Only scream a sharp auditory alarm when danger is **imminent (< 2.5 s)**.
> 4. Quietly illuminate a green symbol when everything is fine.
> A **User-Friendly ADAS Interface** is that co-pilot — *informative but never overwhelming*, *helpful but never intrusive*, *alarming but never panic-inducing*.

The three golden rules of ADAS HMI design are summarized below.

| Rule | Ergonomic Principle | Engineering Consequence |
|------|--------------------|-------------------------|
| **Visibility** | Information must be perceivable in < 200 ms glance time | Display within **±15°** of driver's forward line of sight |
| **Comprehensibility** | Symbols obey **ISO 2575** road vehicle symbols | Standardized iconography reduces interpretation latency |
| **Actionability** | Driver must know *what to do* within **1.2 s** | Multi-modal redundancy (visual + auditory + haptic) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The acceptable **Total Eyes-Off-Road Time (TEORT)** for secondary-task interaction in modern vehicles is **≤ 2.0 seconds per NHTSA Phase 1 guidelines** and **≤ 12 seconds cumulative** for infotainment tasks.

## 1.3 Physical & Perceptual Constants

- **Driver forward useful field of view (UFOV):** **±15° horizontally**, **±5° vertically** at 95th percentile glance detection.
- **Auditory warning intensity:** **75–90 dB(A)** at driver's ear for imminent warnings.
- **Haptic seat/steering wheel alert:** **5–8 Hz vibration** for advisory, **15–30 Hz** for urgent.
- **Driver reaction time to ADAS alert:** mean **1.5 s**, 95th percentile **2.5 s** (Green, 2000).
- **HUD (Head-Up Display) virtual image distance:** **2.0–2.5 m** from driver's eyes (projected beyond the hood).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Theoretical Framework

The theoretical foundation of a User-Friendly ADAS Interface rests on **four pillars** of automotive ergonomics.

### Pillar 1 — Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (MRT)

Wickens' MRT posits that human information processing is divided into independent **perceptual, cognitive, and response** channels. The probability of interference between two tasks depends on whether they share resources.

- **Visual–manual** tasks compete (e.g., reading a screen + pressing a button).
- **Auditory–verbal** tasks are largely independent of visual–manual ones.
- **Haptic** channels are orthogonal to visual and auditory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To minimize driver distraction, ADAS alerts should preferentially use **cross-modal redundancy** (e.g., a visual icon *plus* an auditory tone) rather than overload a single channel.

### Pillar 2 — Driver Workload Management (Stanton & Young Model)

The workload $W$ experienced by a driver is modelled as:

$$
W = f(T_{\text{task}}, C_{\text{available}})
$$

where $T_{\text{task}}$ is the **task demand** and $C_{\text{available}}$ is the **available cognitive capacity**. The ADAS HMI must keep $W$ within the **Yerkes-Dodson optimal band**:

$$
W_{\text{low}} \leq W \leq W_{\text{high}}
$$

Below $W_{\text{low}}$ → driver becomes **bored, under-aroused, loses situation awareness** (vigilance decrement). Above $W_{\text{high}}$ → **cognitive overload**, missed warnings, and increased crash risk.

### Pillar 3 — Trust Calibration Theory (Lee & See, 2004)

Driver trust $T(t)$ evolves dynamically:

$$
\frac{dT}{dt} = \alpha \cdot (P_{\text{perceived}} - P_{\text{actual}}) - \beta \cdot (T - T_{\text{desired}})
$$

where $P_{\text{perceived}}$ is the driver's perceived system performance and $P_{\text{actual}}$ is the true performance. The HMI must communicate **system limitations** honestly to prevent both **disuse** (low trust) and **misuse** (over-trust).

### Pillar 4 — Ecological Interface Design (EID) — Rasmussen & Vicente

The HMI should reveal the **abstraction–decomposition hierarchy** of the vehicle:
- **Physical layer:** Actuators, sensors (raw signals).
- **Functional layer:** Subsystem states (ACC speed, lane offset).
- **Abstract layer:** Intent, goals, planned trajectories.
- **Work domain layer:** Traffic rules, road geometry, weather.

A user-friendly interface exposes the **appropriate layer** to the **appropriate driver state**. For example, in normal driving, only the *abstract* layer is shown. In a takeover request (TOR), the *functional* and *physical* layers are progressively revealed.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Driving Task Completion Time** | $T_{\text{task}} = T_{\text{visual}} + T_{\text{cognitive}} + T_{\text{motor}}$ | seconds | Model Human Processor (Card, Moran & Newell) |
| 2 | **Total Eyes-Off-Road Time Budget** | $\text{TEORT} \leq 2.0\,\text{s}$ (single glance); $\sum \text{TEORT} \leq 12\,\text{s}$ | seconds | NHTSA Phase 1 Guidelines |
| 3 | **Workload Index (NASA-TLX)** | $\text{WL} = \frac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot r_i$ | dimensionless, [0,100] | $w_i$ = subscale weight, $r_i$ = raw rating |
| 4 | **Trust Miscalibration Error** | $E_{\text{trust}} = \vert T_{\text{declared}} - T_{\text{appropriate}} \vert$ | dimensionless | Should be minimized |
| 5 | **Auditory Warning Detection Latency** | $L_{50} = 0.05 \cdot (I - I_{\text{th}})$ | seconds | $I$ = intensity (dB), $I_{\text{th}}$ = threshold |
| 6 | **Haptic Reaction Time (Steering Wheel)** | $RT_{h} = 380 + 12 \cdot (V_{\text{vehicle}} - 60)$ | ms | Approximation; $V$ in km/h |
| 7 | **Glance Frequency Rate** | $G_f = \dfrac{N_{\text{glances}}}{\Delta t}$ | glances/s | Typical $G_f \leq 0.6$ Hz on highway |
| 8 | **Situation Awareness (SAGAT)** | $\text{SA} = \dfrac{N_{\text{correctly answered}}}{N_{\text{total questions}}}$ | ratio [0,1] | Endsley 3-level model |
| 9 | **Information Overload Ratio** | $\text{IOR} = \dfrac{I_{\text{presented}}}{I_{\text{processable}}}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\leq 1.0$ for safe operation |
| 10 | **Takeover Request Time Budget** | $T_{\text{TOR}} \geq 6.0\,\text{s}$ (SAE L3) | seconds | Minimum budget for quality transition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter:** In KTU 14-mark questions, examiners frequently ask to *compute* workload index, evaluate IOR, or justify the choice of a modality for a given alert. Memorize the **TEORT, NASA-TLX formula, and TOR budget** above all others.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above directly drive:
- **Tesla Autopilot HMI** — uses redundant visual + auditory + haptic warnings for hands-off detection.
- **Mercedes DRIVE PILOT (SAE L3)** — uses turquoise light bars, steering wheel LEDs, and seat vibration for status.
- **BMW iDrive 8.5** — uses **natural language voice control** to keep TEORT low.
- **Volvo EX90 LiDAR visualization** — shows "what the car sees" to calibrate trust.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem A — Designing a Warning Hierarchy for a Forward Collision Warning (FCW) System

> **Problem Statement (KTU 14-mark style):** An automotive OEM is designing an FCW system for a mid-size sedan. The HMI team must assign an appropriate warning modality for three escalating threat levels: (i) advisory, (ii) imminent, and (iii) critical. The driver's mean reaction time is **1.5 s**, the auditory detection threshold $I_{\text{th}}$ is **20 dB**, the cabin noise is **55 dB(A)**, and the steering wheel haptic motor operates at **15 Hz**. Evaluate and justify the choice of modality for each threat level.

### Step 1 — Compute the Auditory Latency for a Cabin Warning Tone

We model the auditory detection latency using the linear approximation:

$$
L_{50} = 0.05 \cdot (I - I_{\text{th}})
$$

**Substituting the values:**

$$
L_{50} = 0.05 \cdot (75 - 20) = 0.05 \cdot 55
$$

$$
L_{50} = 2.75\,\text{s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** At 75 dB, the auditory alert is detectable within **2.75 s** of onset. This is *too slow* for a critical warning where the driver must respond in **< 1.5 s**.

### Step 2 — Compute the Steering Wheel Haptic Reaction Time

Using the empirical haptic reaction time model:

$$
RT_{h} = 380 + 12 \cdot (V_{\text{vehicle}} - 60)
$$

**At a cruising speed of 100 km/h:**

$$
RT_{h} = 380 + 12 \cdot (100 - 60) = 380 + 12 \cdot 40
$$

$$
RT_{h} = 380 + 480 = 860\,\text{ms} = 0.86\,\text{s}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The haptic modality produces a response **3.2× faster** than the auditory modality in this case, making it ideal for time-critical alerts.

### Step 3 — Compute the Visual Icon Glance Time

For a glance time on a Head-Up Display (HUD) icon:

$$
T_{\text{visual}} = T_{\text{perception}} + T_{\text{cognition}}
$$

Typical values (Model Human Processor):
- $T_{\text{perception}} = 100\,\text{ms}$
- $T_{\text{cognition}} = 70\,\text{ms}$ (for a familiar icon)

Therefore:

$$
T_{\text{visual}} = 100 + 70 = 170\,\text{ms} = 0.17\,\text{s}
$$

### Step 4 — Construct the Warning Hierarchy Matrix

Using the latency results, we now justify the modality choice:

| Threat Level | Time-to-Collision (TTC) | Primary Modality | Secondary Modality | Justification |
|--------------|------------------------|------------------|--------------------|---------------|
| **Advisory** | TTC $\in [3.0, 5.0]$ s | **Visual HUD icon** (yellow) | Soft chime | Time budget is sufficient; visual alone preserves attention |
| **Imminent** | TTC $\in [1.5, 3.0]$ s | **Auditory tone (85 dB)** | Visual icon (red) | Auditory captures attention across the entire UFOV |
| **Critical** | TTC $< 1.5$ s | **Haptic (15 Hz steering wheel pulse)** | Pre-charge brakes | Fastest reaction (0.86 s); redundant with braking |

### Step 5 — Verify Information Overload Ratio (IOR)

$$
\text{IOR} = \frac{I_{\text{presented}}}{I_{\text{processable}}}
$$

For the **advisory** level, only 1 icon + 1 soft tone is presented; the driver can process $\geq 3$ items/s. Hence:

$$
\text{IOR}_{\text{advisory}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.67 < 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

For the **critical** level, haptic + visual + pre-charge is *complementary*, not redundant, so the effective $I_{\text{presented}}$ is **1.0 equivalent item**:

$$
\text{IOR}_{\text{critical}} = \frac{1.0}{3} = 0.33 < 1.0 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The proposed hierarchy respects Wickens' MRT, achieves safe TEORT, and satisfies ISO 15006/15005 auditory presentation requirements. The haptic channel is reserved for **critical** events, where the **0.86 s** reaction time is essential to avoid collision.

## 3.2 Analytical Problem B — Computing NASA-TLX Workload for ADAS Display Density

> **Problem Statement:** During a highway driving simulation, six participants rate their workload on the NASA-TLX subscales (raw scores out of 100). The pairwise comparison yields the following weights. Compute the **Adjusted Workload Score (AWS)** and comment on whether the ADAS interface is ergonomic.

| Subscale | Mental Demand (MD) | Physical Demand (PD) | Temporal Demand (TD) | Performance (P) | Effort (E) | Frustration (F) |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|----------------------|-----------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Weight (0–1) | 0.25 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.20 | 0.10 |
| Mean Raw Rating | 55 | 20 | 70 | 30 | 60 | 40 |

### Step 1 — Sum the Weights for Verification

$$
\sum w_i = 0.25 + 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00
$$

The weights sum to 1, which is correct.

### Step 2 — Compute the Weighted Sum

$$
\text{AWS} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot r_i
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{AWS} = (0.25 \cdot 55) + (0.10 \cdot 20) + (0.20 \cdot 70) + (0.15 \cdot 30) + (0.20 \cdot 60) + (0.10 \cdot 40)
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\text{AWS} = 13.75 + 2.0 + 14.0 + 4.5 + 12.0 + 4.0
$$

Summing:

$$
\text{AWS} = 50.25
$$

### Step 3 — Ergonomic Interpretation

A NASA-TLX score of **50.25** falls into the **moderate-to-high** workload band.

- $0 \leq \text{AWS} \leq 20$: Very Low Workload
- $20 < \text{AWS} \leq 40$: Low Workload
- $40 < \text{AWS} \leq 60$: **Moderate Workload** ← our case
- $60 < \text{AWS} \leq 80$: High Workload
- $80 < \text{AWS} \leq 100$: Very High Workload

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU):** Students often forget to **invert the Performance subscale** because *lower perceived performance = higher workload*. The raw rating must be subtracted from 100 *before* weighting: $r_{\text{performance, inverted}} = 100 - 30 = 70$. Using the correct value: $(0.15 \cdot 70) = 10.5$ → $\text{AWS} = 56.75$ (High Workload band). The corrected value shows that the interface is *not yet ergonomic* and requires redesign.

## 3.3 Analytical Problem C — Takeover Request (TOR) Time Budget for SAE L3

> **Problem Statement:** A SAE Level 3 automated vehicle must issue a TOR to the driver when it reaches its **Operational Design Domain (ODD)** limit. Given the driver's mean reaction time $RT = 1.5$ s, transition time $T_{\text{transition}} = 4.0$ s, and a safety margin of **0.5 s**, compute the minimum TOR lead time and verify against ISO 21902.

### Step 1 — Apply the TOR Lead-Time Equation

$$
T_{\text{TOR,min}} = RT + T_{\text{transition}} + S_{\text{margin}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{\text{TOR,min}} = 1.5 + 4.0 + 0.5
$$

$$
T_{\text{TOR,min}} = 6.0\,\text{s}
$$

### Step 2 — Verify Against Industry Standards

- **SAE J3016 Annex (informative):** Recommends $\geq 10$ s for *quality* takeover.
- **ISO/TR 21902:2022:** Recommends $T_{\text{TOR,min}} \geq 6.0$ s for *minimum safe* handover.
- **NHTSA AV STEP:** Allows $T_{\text{TOR}} \geq 6.0$ s for highway ODD exit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The computed **6.0 s** matches the ISO minimum. The HMI must issue an **escalating multi-modal TOR** at $t = -6.0$ s and an **emergency haptic alert** at $t = -2.0$ s.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ADAS HMI Functional Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid

    percMod["PERCEPTION MODULE"]:::perception
    planMod["PLANNING MODULE"]:::planning
    ctrlMod["CONTROL MODULE"]:::control
    hmiCore["HMI CENTRAL PROCESSOR"]:::core
    visDis["VISUAL DISPLAYS"]:::visual
    audDis["AUDITORY ALERTS"]:::auditory
    hapDis["HAPTIC FEEDBACK"]:::haptic
    drvMon["DRIVER MONITORING CAMERA"]:::monitor
    hmiCore -->|Filtered State| visDis
    hmiCore -->|Tone Trigger| audDis
    hmiCore -->|Vibration Pattern| hapDis
    planMod -->|Tor Event| hmiCore
    ctrlMod -->|Intervention Request| hmiCore
    percMod -->|Object Detection| hmiCore
    drvMon -->|Gaze Attention| hmiCore
    classDef perception fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#3B6FB6,color:#0B2545
    classDef planning fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B68800,color:#3D2C00

```

> **Reading the diagram:** The HMI Central Processor fuses data from perception, planning, control, and the Driver Monitoring Camera (DMS) into three output channels — **visual, auditory, and haptic** — that respect the multiple-resource principle.

## 4.2 Multi-Modal Warning Escalation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["ADAS TRIGGER EVENT"]:::start
    lvl1["LEVEL 1 ADVISORY TTC 3 to 5 s"]:::advisory
    lvl2["LEVEL 2 IMMINENT TTC 1.5 to 3 s"]:::imminent
    lvl3["LEVEL 3 CRITICAL TTC less than 1.5 s"]:::critical
    endA["DRIVER RESPONSE OR AUTO INTERVENTION"]:::endN
    startA --> lvl1
    lvl1 -->|no response| lvl2
    lvl2 -->|no response| lvl3
    lvl3 --> endA
    classDef start fill:#222,stroke:#222,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef advisory fill:#FFF8C5,stroke:#C9A227,color:#3D2C00
    classDef imminent fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#C96A27,color:#3D1F00
    classDef critical fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#B6344A,color:#4A0E1C
    classDef endN fill:#1F8A4C,stroke:#1F8A4C,color:#FFFFFF
```

## 4.3 ADAS Information Abstraction Hierarchy (EID Layers)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["WORK DOMAIN LAYER Road Rules Weather Traffic"]:::l1
    layer2["ABSTRACT LAYER Intent Goal Destination"]:::l2
    layer3["FUNCTIONAL LAYER ACC Speed Lane Offset Distance"]:::l3
    layer4["PHYSICAL LAYER Brake Pressure Steering Angle Sensor Raw Data"]:::l4
    driver["DRIVER"]:::drv
    driver -->|Normal Mode reads| layer2
    driver -->|Tor Mode reads| layer3
    driver -->|Diagnostic Mode reads| layer4
    layer1 -.->|Constraints| layer2
    classDef l1 fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#3B6FB6,color:#0B2545
    classDef l2 fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B68800,color:#3D2C00
    classDef l3 fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#B6344A,color:#4A0E1C
    classDef l4 fill:#EFE3FF,stroke:#6A3FB6,color:#2A0E4A
    classDef drv fill:#E0FFE5,stroke:#1F8A4C,color:#0E3D22
```

> **Interpretation:** In a *user-friendly* ADAS, the HMI defaults to the **Abstract Layer** (high-level, non-distracting). When the driver must take over, the HMI progressively reveals the **Functional** and (rarely) the **Physical** layers — minimizing information overload.

---
