<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Define the term "Human-Machine Interface (HMI)" in the context of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems. List any two ergonomic standards that govern its design.**

**Model Answer:**

A Human-Machine Interface (HMI) in ADAS is the integrated set of displays, controls, and feedback modalities that enable bidirectional information exchange between the driver and the vehicle's automated driving subsystems. It includes visual displays (instrument cluster, HUD, center-stack), auditory alerts (tones, voice prompts), and haptic actuators (steering wheel, pedals, seat).

Two relevant ergonomic standards are:
1. **ISO 15005:2017** — specifies ergonomic requirements for *in-vehicle auditory presentation*.
2. **ISO 2575:2010** — defines *symbols for controls, indicators, and tell-tales* in road vehicles.
3. (Bonus) **NHTSA Driver Distraction Guidelines** and **ISO 16673** (occlusion method).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the "Total Eyes-Off-Road Time (TEORT)" limit as prescribed by NHTSA for secondary tasks in modern vehicles? Why is it critical for ADAS HMI design?**

**Model Answer:**

According to the **NHTSA Phase 1 Driver Distraction Guidelines**, the **TEORT for a single glance** must be $\leq 2.0$ seconds, and the **cumulative TEORT for any infotainment task** must be $\leq 12$ seconds.

It is critical for ADAS HMI design because:
1. Visual eyes-off-road time is the single strongest predictor of crash risk — each 1 s increase in TEORT raises near-crash odds by ~2.5×.
2. ADAS often runs in the *peripheral* attention mode; the HMI must therefore be *glanceable* (comprehendible in < 1.2 s).
3. Exceeding TEORT while ACC or LKA is engaged can cause the driver to be classified as "out-of-the-loop", leading to dangerous disengagement transitions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Module 3 Internal Choice, Part B*

**(a)** Explain the **Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory** with a neat sketch of the four-dimensional resource model. Discuss how it informs the choice of warning modality in a Forward Collision Warning (FCW) system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SAE Level 3 automated vehicle must hand over control to the driver when it exits its Operational Design Domain. Given:
- Mean driver reaction time $RT = 1.5$ s
- Manual transition time $T_{\text{trans}} = 4.0$ s
- Safety margin $S = 0.5$ s
- Current vehicle speed $V = 90$ km/h

Compute the **minimum Takeover Request lead-time**, justify whether it is sufficient under **ISO 21902**, and design an **escalating multi-modal TOR sequence** (visual + auditory + haptic) with specific frequency/intensity values. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Definition of MRT:** Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (1980, 2008) posits that human information processing is distributed across **four orthogonal dimensions** of resources:
1. **Processing stages** — Perceptual vs. Cognitive vs. Response.
2. **Perceptual modalities** — Visual vs. Auditory.
3. **Visual channels** — Focal vs. Peripheral.
4. **Response modalities** — Manual vs. Speech vs. Oculomotor.

**Neat sketch (textual representation):**

| Stage \ Modality | Visual-Focal | Visual-Peripheral | Auditory | Manual | Speech |
|------------------|--------------|--------------------|----------|--------|--------|
| Perceptual       | (high load)  | (medium load)      | (low)    | —      | —      |
| Cognitive        | (high load)  | (medium load)      | (low)    | —      | —      |
| Response         | —            | (eye movement)     | —        | (high) | (low)  |

**Application to FCW modality choice:**

- For an **advisory FCW** (TTC = 3–5 s), use *visual focal* (HUD icon) so the driver can continue forward gaze.
- For an **imminent FCW** (TTC = 1.5–3 s), shift to *auditory* so the alert reaches the driver without requiring eye movement (auditory is processed peripherally).
- For a **critical FCW** (TTC < 1.5 s), engage *haptic* (steering wheel vibration at 15 Hz) — the haptic response channel is **orthogonal** to the visual-manual channel that the driver is already using, achieving **cross-modal redundancy** with minimum interference.

> **Conclusion:** MRT dictates that *no two alerts should compete for the same resource pool*. The HMI should stage the alert across **orthogonal channels** to maximize driver response speed and minimize cognitive tunneling.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute minimum TOR lead time:**

$$
T_{\text{TOR,min}} = RT + T_{\text{trans}} + S
$$

$$
T_{\text{TOR,min}} = 1.5 + 4.0 + 0.5 = 6.0\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — ISO 21902 compliance check:**

ISO/TR 21902:2022 specifies $T_{\text{TOR,min}} \geq 6.0$ s for a *minimum safe* takeover. Our computed value **exactly matches** the ISO minimum. However, SAE J3016 (informative) recommends $\geq 10$ s for a *quality* transition. The system is therefore **legally compliant but not best-practice**.

**Step 3 — Design the escalating multi-modal TOR sequence:**

| Time Before ODD Exit (s) | Visual Modality | Auditory Modality | Haptic Modality |
|---------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| $t = -6.0$ | **Turquoise light bar** illuminates; "Automation disengaging in 6 s" message on HUD | None (silent advisory) | None |
| $t = -4.0$ | **Amber (yellow) cluster warning icon** + countdown timer | **Single chime** (75 dB(A), 500 Hz) | Soft seat pulse (5 Hz) |
| $t = -2.0$ | **Red urgent icon** + bold "TAKE OVER NOW" | **Double-beep urgent tone** (85 dB(A), 1000 Hz) | **Steering wheel vibration** at 15 Hz, 50% duty |
| $t = -0.5$ | **Full red** display; "EMERGENCY — DRIVE NOW" | **Continuous tone** (90 dB(A)) | **Strong vibration** at 25 Hz, 100% duty |
| $t = 0$ | **Minimal Risk Maneuver (MRM)** activation: vehicle pulls to hard shoulder | (Continued) | (Continued) |

**Step 4 — Justification of values:**

- **Auditory intensity (75–90 dB):** 75 dB exceeds the cabin 55 dB noise floor by 20 dB — a comfortable detection margin per ISO 15006.
- **Haptic frequencies:** 5 Hz = advisory (low urgency, 8% driver reaction in < 1 s); 15 Hz = alert (cognitive urgency); 25 Hz = alarm (motor urgency, fastest reaction per NHTSA studies).
- **Multi-modal staging:** Each escalation level engages a *new* perceptual channel, ensuring **uninterrupted attention capture** even if the driver is drowsy or distracted.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B:** Students often forget to **invert the NASA-TLX Performance subscale** (Q.A-a type). They also confuse *forward collision warning* (FCW, SAE J3063) with *automatic emergency braking* (AEB, which is an *intervention*, not a warning). Ensure your answer explicitly distinguishes **warning** (driver responds) from **intervention** (system acts). Failing to do so costs **1–2 marks** consistently.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Module 3 Internal Choice, Part B*

**(a)** Define **trust calibration** in the context of ADAS. With a sketch, explain the **Lee & See trust dynamics model**, and discuss how the HMI can be designed to keep trust within the **appropriate band** (avoiding both *disuse* and *misuse*). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive UX team is evaluating two candidate HMI designs — **Design X (visual-only icons on a center-stack touchscreen)** and **Design Y (Head-Up Display + auditory + haptic redundant alerts)** — using NASA-TLX. The weighted subscale scores are:

| Subscale | MD | PD | TD | P (inverted) | E | F |
|----------|----|----|----|--------------|---|---|
| **Design X** Weight | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.10 | 0.25 | 0.10 |
| **Design X** Raw | 70 | 30 | 65 | 25 | 70 | 50 |
| **Design Y** Weight | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.20 | 0.25 | 0.15 | 0.10 |
| **Design Y** Raw | 30 | 25 | 40 | 75 | 35 | 25 |

Compute the **Adjusted Workload Score** for both designs. Which design is more ergonomic, and why? Justify with reference to the **TEORT** criterion. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Trust Calibration Definition:** Trust calibration in ADAS refers to the alignment between the **driver's subjective trust** in an automated system and the **objective trustworthiness** (i.e., true capability) of that system.

**Lee & See trust dynamics model (2004):**

The model comprises three components:
1. **Analytical trust** — based on the driver's *beliefs* about system performance.
2. **Experiential (or analog) trust** — based on the driver's *feelings* gained through repeated use.
3. **Situational trust** — the *state-dependent* component modulated by workload, fatigue, and risk.

**Textual sketch of the dynamic equation:**

$$
T(t+1) = T(t) + \alpha \cdot (P_{\text{perceived}} - P_{\text{actual}}) - \beta \cdot (T(t) - T_{\text{desired}}) + \gamma \cdot \text{Anthropomorphism}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = sensitivity to performance discrepancy
- $\beta$ = rate of trust *recovery* after failure
- $\gamma$ = anthropomorphism factor (human-like voice raises trust)

**HMI design strategies to keep trust in the appropriate band:**

| Problem | Cause | HMI Solution |
|---------|-------|--------------|
| **Misuse (over-trust)** | Driver is unaware of system limits | **Disclose ODD boundaries** via HUD "available range" graphic; gentle "are you sure?" voice prompts for risky handovers |
| **Disuse (under-trust)** | Driver experienced a false alarm | **Calibrate alert sensitivity**; provide a "system confidence" meter (Green/Yellow/Red) to inform driver of the system's *belief* state |
| **Trust decay (over time)** | Driver stops paying attention | **Engagement pulse** — periodic gentle haptic on the steering wheel reminding driver to keep hands on wheel |

**Summary:** The HMI must communicate *what the system is doing*, *why it is doing it*, and *what it cannot do* — three things simultaneously — to keep trust in the **calibrated band**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b)

**Step 1 — Compute AWS for Design X:**

$$
\text{AWS}_X = (0.30 \cdot 70) + (0.10 \cdot 30) + (0.15 \cdot 65) + (0.10 \cdot 25) + (0.25 \cdot 70) + (0.10 \cdot 50)
$$

$$
\text{AWS}_X = 21.0 + 3.0 + 9.75 + 2.5 + 17.5 + 5.0
$$

$$
\text{AWS}_X = 58.75
$$

**Step 2 — Compute AWS for Design Y:**

$$
\text{AWS}_Y = (0.20 \cdot 30) + (0.10 \cdot 25) + (0.20 \cdot 40) + (0.25 \cdot 75) + (0.15 \cdot 35) + (0.10 \cdot 25)
$$

$$
\text{AWS}_Y = 6.0 + 2.5 + 8.0 + 18.75 + 5.25 + 2.5
$$

$$
\text{AWS}_Y = 43.00
$$

**Step 3 — Compare against the Workload Band:**

| Design | AWS | Band | Ergonomic Verdict |
|--------|-----|------|-------------------|
| **Design X** | 58.75 | High (60 > 58.75) — borderline High Workload | Marginal — too much visual/manual demand |
| **Design Y** | 43.00 | Moderate Workload | **Acceptable** |

**Step 4 — TEORT Justification:**

- **Design X** requires the driver to take **multiple long glances** to a center-stack touchscreen located $\approx 30°$ from the forward line of sight. The cumulative TEORT easily exceeds NHTSA's 12 s cumulative budget on a 30-min drive. The Performance subscale is *very low* (25/100 after inversion, i.e., driver feels unsafe) — confirming over-load.
- **Design Y** uses the **HUD (within $\pm 5°$ of forward sight)** for visual alerts, supplemented by auditory + haptic channels. The driver can keep eyes on the road **> 95%** of the time. TEORT drops to **$\leq 1.0$ s per glance** — well below the 2.0 s limit.

**Conclusion:** **Design Y is more ergonomic** and is recommended for production. It respects Wickens' Multiple Resource Theory (cross-modal redundancy) and complies with the NHTSA TEORT budget.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Part B Question B:** A common mistake is forgetting to **invert the Performance subscale** — *high raw rating* on Performance means *low workload*, so it must be flipped to $(100 - r_{\text{performance}})$ *before* weighting. In the table above, Design Y's raw 75 was already inverted for you; if you apply the weight to 75 directly, you will get a wrong (lower) AWS for Design Y and mis-identify the ergonomic winner. **Always re-read the problem to see if the table gives raw or pre-inverted Performance.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — User-Friendly Interfaces for ADAS**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **HMI = Visual + Auditory + Haptic** — three orthogonal channels per Wickens' MRT.
- ☐ **TEORT limits:** $\leq 2.0$ s single glance, $\leq 12$ s cumulative (NHTSA Phase 1).
- ☐ **NASA-TLX** uses 6 subscales; **invert Performance** before weighting.
- ☐ **TOR lead time** $\geq 6.0$ s (ISO 21902) and $\geq 10$ s (SAE J3016, quality handover).
- ☐ **Auditory warnings:** 75–90 dB(A) at driver's ear, ahead of cabin noise floor by 20 dB.
- ☐ **Haptic frequencies:** 5 Hz (advisory), 15 Hz (alert), 25 Hz (alarm).
- ☐ **HUD virtual image:** 2.0–2.5 m from driver's eyes, within $\pm 15°$ horizontal UFOV.
- ☐ **Trust calibration** = subjective trust aligned with objective system capability.
- ☐ **Workload band:** keep driver in **40–60 NASA-TLX** sweet spot.
- ☐ **Iconography standards:** ISO 2575 (road vehicle symbols).

### High-Yield Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ $L_{50} = 0.05 \cdot (I - I_{\text{th}})$
- ☐ $RT_h = 380 + 12 \cdot (V - 60)$ ms
- ☐ $T_{\text{TOR,min}} = RT + T_{\text{trans}} + S$
- ☐ $\text{AWS} = \sum w_i \cdot r_i$ (with Performance inverted)
- ☐ $\text{IOR} = I_{\text{presented}} / I_{\text{processable}} \leq 1.0$

### Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid
- ☐ **Don't forget to invert the Performance subscale** in NASA-TLX.
- ☐ **Don't confuse warning (FCW)** with **intervention (AEB)**.
- ☐ **Don't claim TEORT is for a single task** — it is **cumulative across the drive**.
- ☐ **Don't use only visual alerts** — NHTSA requires **redundancy for critical alerts**.
- ☐ **Don't design for SAE L2 and L3 with the same HMI** — L3 needs a takeover *handshake*, not a passive alert.

### Standards to Cite
- ☐ **ISO 15005** (dialogue management)
- ☐ **ISO 15006** (auditory presentation)
- ☐ **ISO 16673** (occlusion method)
- ☐ **ISO 2575** (symbol library)
- ☐ **ISO/TR 21902:2022** (TOR for automated vehicles)
- ☐ **SAE J3016** (driving automation levels)
- ☐ **SAE J3063** (active safety systems terminology)
- ☐ **FMVSS 101-E / NHTSA Phase 1** (driver distraction)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Haptic Feedback and Tactile Controls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Haptic Feedback and Tactile Controls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Haptic Feedback** refers to the use of mechanical vibrations, forces, or motions relayed to the user through a control interface (steering wheel, pedal, touchscreen, seat, or gear shifter) to communicate vehicle state, alert the driver, or confirm an input action. **Tactile Controls** are user-interface elements specifically engineered to exploit the human sense of touch (somatosensory system) for the purpose of blind operation — allowing a driver to identify, locate, and manipulate vehicle functions without diverting visual attention from the road.

In ISO 9241-960 / ISO/TS 13861 terminology adopted by the KTU 2024 scheme:

$$
H = f(M,\ K,\ T,\ F_{n})
$$

where:
- $H$ = Haptic perception index (driver-perceived magnitude)
- $M$ = Modal stimulus (vibration, force, displacement, texture)
- $K$ = Cognitive loading factor during stimulus onset
- $T$ = Transmission pathway stiffness/damping
- $F_{n}$ = Normalised force vector transmitted to skin receptors

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Piano Player"

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy for First-Time Learners**
> Imagine a **piano player wearing a blindfold**. Even without seeing the keys, the player can identify each note by the *shape*, *spacing*, and *resistance* of the keys under the fingertips. This is **tactile discrimination**.
>
> Now imagine the same player pressing a key and feeling a *gentle buzz* through the key whenever a wrong note is detected — this is **haptic feedback**.
>
> In an automobile, the steering wheel, gear knob, and touchscreen function exactly like this piano: every surface is engineered so the driver can "feel" the right button, and every action is rewarded (or warned) with a carefully tuned vibration/force.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT] **Threshold Values to Memorise for KTU Board Exam**
> | Parameter | Standard Human Threshold | Automotive Application Range |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Vibration Frequency (skin) | **0.4 Hz – 1000 Hz** | **5 Hz – 80 Hz** (steering wheel) |
> | Minimum detectable displacement | **\geq 0.01 mm** | 0.05 mm – 1.5 mm |
> | Minimum detectable force | **\geq 1 mN** | 0.5 N – 12 N (rotary knobs) |
> | Reaction latency for hazard alert | **\leq 250 ms** | **\leq 100 ms** (ISO 2575) |
> | Acceptable touchscreen force | **0.2 N – 1.5 N** | 0.5 N – 1.2 N (UN R121) |

## 1.4 Why Haptics is a *Pillar* of Automotive Ergonomics

The human somatosensory cortex processes tactile information in **\leq 30 ms**, while the visual cortex takes **150 – 250 ms** to register a road hazard. Hence, haptic channels drastically reduce **eyes-off-road time (EORT)**, directly supporting the KTU 2024 Course Outcome:

> **CO3:** *Apply ergonomic principles in the design of vehicle interfaces to enhance driver safety, comfort, and situational awareness.*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Functional Pillars of Haptic Interaction

### Pillar A — **Tactile Feedback (Cutaneous Stimulation)**
This activates the skin's mechanoreceptors (Meissner, Pacinian, Merkel, Ruffini endings). Each receptor responds to a specific frequency band:

$$
f_{M} \approx 30\ \text{Hz}\ \text{(Merkel — pressure/edge)} \qquad f_{P} \approx 250\ \text{Hz}\ \text{(Pacinian — vibration/buzz)}
$$

### Pillar B — **Kinesthetic / Force Feedback (Proprioceptive Stimulation)**
This activates muscles, tendons, and joints to communicate resistance, weight, or position. Example: Variable-ratio electric power steering giving a "heavier" feel at highway speeds.

## 2.2 The Three Operational Layers

1. **Input Layer** — Physical event (lane departure, gear engaged, button pressed).
2. **Processing Layer** — ECU/HMI controller maps the event to a predefined haptic profile.
3. **Output Layer** — Actuator (ERM motor, LRA, piezoelectric, voice-coil) reproduces the profile.

## 2.3 Mathematical Foundation of a Tactile Vibration

A vibrotactile stimulus delivered by an **Eccentric Rotating Mass (ERM)** actuator is modelled as:

$$
x(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

where:
- $A$ = Peak displacement amplitude (mm)
- $f$ = Drive frequency (Hz)
- $\phi$ = Phase offset (rad)

The acceleration transmitted to the skin (which is what Pacinian corpuscles actually detect) is:

$$
a(t) = \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = -(2\pi f)^{2} \cdot A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

The peak acceleration magnitude is therefore:

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = (2\pi f)^{2} \cdot A
$$

## 2.4 Force Feedback Equation (Steering Wheel Context)

For a steering-column-mounted active feedback motor, the resistive torque delivered is:

$$
\tau_{fb} = K_{p} \cdot \theta_{err} + K_{d} \cdot \dot{\theta}_{err}
$$

where:
- $\tau_{fb}$ = Feedback torque at the steering rim (N·m)
- $K_{p}$ = Proportional gain (N·m / rad)
- $K_{d}$ = Derivative gain (N·m·s / rad)
- $\theta_{err}$ = Angular error between driver input and reference lane trajectory (rad)

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Memorise This Table Before Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Value (Automotive) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $a_{peak} = (2\pi f)^{2} A$ | Peak vibration acceleration | 0.5 – 12 m/s² |
| 2 | $\tau_{fb} = K_{p} \theta + K_{d} \dot{\theta}$ | Steering torque feedback | 1.5 – 8 N·m |
| 3 | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of LRA | 150 – 250 Hz |
| 4 | $Z = \sqrt{R^{2} + (X_{L} - X_{C})^{2}}$ | Piezo impedance | 50 – 500 Ω |
| 5 | $T_{react} = t_{stim} + t_{prop} + t_{cog}$ | Total driver reaction time | **\leq 700 ms** |
| 6 | $SNR_{haptic} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S_{stim}}{N_{noise}}\right)$ | Haptic signal clarity ratio | **\geq 15 dB** |
| 7 | $F_{perceived} = F_{applied} \cdot \eta_{trans}$ | Force felt at skin | $\eta_{trans} \approx 0.65$ |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in the Real World

Haptic systems reduce driver cognitive load by **up to 27 %** (NHTSA Eye Glance Studies) and are mandatory in:
- **LDW** (Lane Departure Warning) — steering wheel rumble
- **BSW** (Blind Spot Warning) — seat vibration
- **FCW** (Forward Collision Warning) — pedal pulsation
- **Touchscreen confirmation** — haptic click on rotary displays (MBUX, iDrive)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation — Peak Acceleration from a Tactile Actuator

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle's steering-wheel haptic motor produces a vertical sinusoidal displacement of peak amplitude **A = 0.4 mm** at a frequency of **f = 35 Hz** to alert the driver of lane drift. Compute the peak acceleration transmitted to the driver's palm.

### Step 1 — Define the displacement function

$$
x(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

Substituting $A = 0.4\ \text{mm}$ and $f = 35\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
x(t) = 0.4 \cdot \sin(70\pi \cdot t)\ \ \text{[mm]}
$$

### Step 2 — First derivative (velocity)

$$
v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = A \cdot 2\pi f \cdot \cos(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

$$
v(t) = 0.4 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 70\pi \cdot \cos(70\pi t)
$$

### Step 3 — Second derivative (acceleration)

$$
a(t) = \frac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = -A \cdot (2\pi f)^{2} \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)
$$

### Step 4 — Extract peak magnitude (sinusoid maximum is unity)

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = A \cdot (2\pi f)^{2}
$$

### Step 5 — Numerical evaluation

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = (0.4 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (2\pi \cdot 35)^{2}
$$

Convert each part:

$$
2\pi \cdot 35 = 219.91\ \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
(2\pi \cdot 35)^{2} = 219.91^{2} = 48{,}360.6\ \text{rad}^{2}\!\!/\text{s}^{2}
$$

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = 0.0004 \cdot 48{,}360.6 = 19.34\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Convert to *g* (1 g = 9.81 m/s²)

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = \frac{19.34}{9.81} \approx 1.97\ g
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Interpretation:** This value lies within the **Pacinian corpuscle's optimal detection window (1 g – 5 g at 30 – 80 Hz)**, hence the driver will reliably perceive the warning.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Steering Feedback Torque for a Lane-Keeping System

**Problem Setup:** A lane-keeping assist ECU computes an angular error $\theta_{err} = 0.05\ \text{rad}$ and angular velocity error $\dot{\theta}_{err} = 0.2\ \text{rad/s}$. Given $K_{p} = 80\ \text{N·m/rad}$ and $K_{d} = 12\ \text{N·m·s/rad}$, compute the corrective torque.

### Step 1 — Write the PD controller law

$$
\tau_{fb} = K_{p} \cdot \theta_{err} + K_{d} \cdot \dot{\theta}_{err}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute numerical values

$$
\tau_{fb} = 80 \cdot 0.05 + 12 \cdot 0.2
$$

### Step 3 — Compute each term

$$
\text{Term 1} = 80 \cdot 0.05 = 4.0\ \text{N·m}
$$

$$
\text{Term 2} = 12 \cdot 0.2 = 2.4\ \text{N·m}
$$

### Step 4 — Sum

$$
\tau_{fb} = 4.0 + 2.4 = 6.4\ \text{N·m}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight:** This torque is well within the safe driver override limit (typically **\leq 8 N·m** under UN R79 lane-keeping regulation), meaning the driver can always overpower the system.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix of Haptic Actuator Technologies

> [!IMPORTANT] **Examiner's Favourite Comparison Table**

| Property | ERM Motor | LRA (Linear Resonant Actuator) | Piezoelectric | Voice-Coil |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency bandwidth | 50 – 200 Hz | 150 – 250 Hz | 100 – 500 Hz | 20 – 1000 Hz |
| Response time | 30 – 50 ms | **\leq 5 ms** | **\leq 1 ms** | 5 – 15 ms |
| Power consumption | High | Medium | **Very Low** | Medium |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High | Medium |
| Automotive use | Steering wheel | Touchscreen | Premium displays | Seat / pedal |
| Haptic realism | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — Haptic Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Driver Event - Lane Drift - Button Press - Hazard] --> nodeB[HMI Controller ECU]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Haptic Profile Mapping Table]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Signal Amplifier Stage]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Actuator Bank - ERM - LRA - Piezo - Voice Coil]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Mechanical Coupling - Steering - Seat - Pedal - Screen]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Driver Somatosensory System]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Cognitive Response]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Corrective Action]
    nodeI --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Tactile Touchscreen Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeT1[Finger Touch Input] --> nodeT2[Capacitive Sensing Layer]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3[Haptic Processor DSP]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4{Control Mode Selector}
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5[Virtual Button Click]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT6[Slider Texture Feedback]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT7[Slider Detent Feedback]
    nodeT5 --> nodeT8[Piezo Actuator Array]
    nodeT6 --> nodeT8
    nodeT7 --> nodeT8
    nodeT8 --> nodeT9[Finger Skin Receptors]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Automotive Haptic Modalities

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeH[Haptic Feedback in Vehicles]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Vibrotactile]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Force Feedback]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[Thermal]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[Electrotactile]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH1a[Steering Wheel Vibration]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH1b[Seat Cushion Buzz]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH1c[Pedal Pulse]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH2a[Steering Resistance]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH2b[Brake-by-Wire Pedal Feel]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH2c[Drive Mode Selector Detent]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH3a[Heated Steering Alert]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH3b[Cooled Seat Warning]
    nodeH4 --> nodeH4a[Experimental Driver Alert]
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Diagram — Tactile Button Confirmation Pulse

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[User Taps Surface] --> nodeS2[Touch Coordinates Validated]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3[ECU Generates Pulse Profile]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4[Driver Amplifier Stage]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Piezo Stack Excites at 200 Hz]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Damped Sine Burst 30 ms]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[User Feels Sharp Click]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define *haptic feedback*. List **two** automotive applications where haptic feedback reduces driver visual distraction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Haptic feedback is the use of **mechanical vibration, force, or motion** delivered through a vehicle control surface (steering wheel, seat, pedal, touchscreen) to communicate information to the driver through the **somatosensory channel** rather than visual or auditory channels.

Two automotive applications:
1. **Lane Departure Warning (LDW):** A 30 – 35 Hz rumble in the steering wheel alerts the driver without requiring a dashboard glance.
2. **Blind Spot Warning (BSW):** Vibration in the driver-seat cushion (typically 50 – 60 Hz) signals an unseen vehicle in the adjacent lane, again preserving forward visual attention.

> **Marking:** Definition – 1 mark; Each application – 1 mark.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** State the **human vibration-detection threshold frequency range** and explain why this range is critical for steering-wheel haptic design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The human hand can perceive vibrotactile stimuli most sensitively in the range **20 Hz – 80 Hz**, with peak sensitivity near **250 Hz** for the Pacinian corpuscles. However, the **5 Hz – 80 Hz** band is preferred for steering-wheel design because:
1. It matches the **natural mechanical resonance of the human hand–steering-wheel coupling**.
2. Lower frequencies are perceived as a *clear rumble* rather than a *buzz*, which is intuitively associated with road feedback.
3. It avoids interference with steering-wheel audio speakers that operate above 100 Hz.

> **Marking:** Range – 1 mark; Two reasons – 1 mark each.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Common mistakes students make in this module:
> - Confusing *tactile* (cutaneous) feedback with *force / kinesthetic* feedback.
> - Writing generic definitions without applying the equation $a_{peak} = (2\pi f)^{2} A$.
> - Forgetting to mention **UN R79** and **UN R121** regulations.
> - Mixing up ERM and LRA actuator characteristics.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Haptic Feedback Design for Driver Alerting

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **operational architecture of a steering-wheel haptic warning system** used in Lane Departure Warning (LDW). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A steering-wheel haptic motor operates at a peak amplitude of **0.6 mm** and a frequency of **40 Hz**. Calculate the peak acceleration transmitted to the driver and express it in *g*. Comment on whether this value lies within the recommended driver-perception window. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Camera / Lane Sensor] → [ECU Decision Logic] → [Haptic Profile Mapper]
        → [PWM Driver Amplifier] → [ERM / LRA Actuator]
        → [Steering Wheel Rim] → [Driver's Palm] → [Driver Response]
```

**2. Functional Description (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Sensing:** A forward-facing camera detects lane markings; deviation beyond a threshold angle (typically **0.15 rad**) triggers the warning logic.
- **Step 2 — Decision:** The ADAS ECU classifies the event severity and selects a pre-stored haptic profile (frequency, amplitude, duration).
- **Step 3 — Drive Stage:** A PWM-controlled H-bridge amplifier supplies current to an ERM or LRA mounted inside the steering-wheel rim.
- **Step 4 — Mechanical Output:** The actuator produces a **vibrotactile signal at 30 – 40 Hz** with peak amplitude between **0.3 mm and 0.7 mm**.
- **Step 5 — Perception:** The Pacinian and Meissner corpuscles in the driver's palm detect the vibration; cognitive processing triggers a corrective steering input.
- **Step 6 — Latency Budget:** Total end-to-end delay is maintained **\leq 250 ms** per ISO 2575.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

$$
A = 0.6\ \text{mm} = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}, \quad f = 40\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the peak acceleration formula:**

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = (2\pi f)^{2} \cdot A
$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular frequency squared:**

$$
(2\pi \cdot 40)^{2} = (80\pi)^{2} = 6400\pi^{2}
$$

$$
6400\pi^{2} \approx 6400 \times 9.8696 \approx 63{,}165\ \text{rad}^{2}\!\!/\text{s}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by amplitude:**

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = 0.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 63{,}165 \approx 37.9\ \text{m/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to g:**

$$
a_{\text{peak}} = \frac{37.9}{9.81} \approx 3.86\ g
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on perception window:**

> The Pacinian corpuscle's optimal detection window is **1 g – 5 g at 30 – 80 Hz**. Since 3.86 g lies within this range, **the warning will be clearly perceivable** without being uncomfortable. **[Full 7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Tactile Controls in Modern Vehicle HMI

**(a)** Explain the **classification of tactile controls** used in modern automotive HMI, with **two examples for each class**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare **ERM**, **LRA**, and **Piezoelectric** haptic actuators on the basis of **frequency response, response time, and cost**. Recommend the most suitable actuator for a **vehicle touchscreen with click feedback**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks

Tactile controls in automotive HMI are classified by the **type of stimulus** they deliver:

**1. Vibrotactile Controls (cutaneous vibration)** — 2 marks
- Example 1: Steering-wheel rumble strip for LDW.
- Example 2: Touchscreen click confirmation pulse.

**2. Force / Kinesthetic Controls (resistance / detent)** — 2 marks
- Example 1: Variable-ratio electric power steering.
- Example 2: Haptic rotary knob (e.g., iDrive controller).

**3. Thermal Controls (temperature shift)** — 1.5 marks
- Example 1: Heated steering wheel indicating an alert.
- Example 2: Cooled seat alert for drowsiness.

**4. Surface-Texture Controls (friction modulation)** — 1.5 marks
- Example 1: Programmable texture on a touchscreen.
- Example 2: Electrostatic-friction display.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks

| Property | ERM | LRA | Piezoelectric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Frequency response | 50 – 200 Hz | 150 – 250 Hz | 100 – 500 Hz |
| Response time | 30 – 50 ms | \leq 5 ms | \leq 1 ms |
| Relative cost | Low | Medium | High |

**Recommendation:** A piezoelectric actuator is best suited for a **vehicle touchscreen with click feedback** because:
1. Its **response time \leq 1 ms** delivers a crisp "click" perception.
2. **Wide bandwidth (100 – 500 Hz)** allows rich texture rendering.
3. **Low power consumption** suits always-on infotainment use.
4. **Compact form factor** integrates beneath the display stack.

**[Full 7 marks awarded for table (4) + justification (3)]**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Board**

- Haptic feedback uses **mechanical vibration / force** to communicate via the **somatosensory channel**.
- Tactile controls enable **eyes-off-road operation** of vehicle functions.
- Two pillars: **Cutaneous (tactile)** and **Proprioceptive (force / kinesthetic)**.
- Core equation: $a_{peak} = (2\pi f)^{2} A$ — peak vibration acceleration.
- Steering feedback equation: $\tau_{fb} = K_{p} \theta + K_{d} \dot{\theta}$.
- Optimal driver-detection band: **20 – 80 Hz**, **1 – 5 g**.
- Latency target: **\leq 250 ms** per ISO 2575.
- Driver torque override limit: **\leq 8 N·m** per UN R79.
- Actuator comparison: ERM = cheap but slow; LRA = fast & efficient; Piezo = premium quality.
- Haptic feedback reduces **EORT** (eyes-off-road time) by up to **27 %**.
- Always mention **ISO 2575** and **UN R79 / R121** in regulatory answers.
- Tactile controls are essential to **CO3** of OEMUT722 (KTU 2024 scheme).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ergonomics in Crash Safety - Crash Test Dummies and Ergonomic Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ergonomics in Crash Safety - Crash Test Dummies and Ergonomic Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD):** A highly instrumented, biofidelic mechanical surrogate of the human body used in vehicular crashworthiness evaluations to measure biomechanical loads, accelerations, and deflections at critical anatomical regions so as to quantify occupant injury risk.

In KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, the ATD is the bridge between **occupant ergonomics** (the human-machine fit inside the vehicle) and **passive safety engineering** (the vehicle's ability to protect the occupant during a crash). The dummy itself is engineered from aluminium, steel, vinyl, and rubber components whose mechanical response mimics human tissue under dynamic loading between **0.5 g** and **80 g**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Ergonomic Data (Crash Context):** Quantitative datasets — including anthropometric dimensions, joint ranges of motion, tissue tolerance limits, and force-deflection responses — that are translated into measurable engineering parameters (accelerations, forces, moments, displacements) on the ATD.

The two flagship categories of ergonomic data used in crash safety are:

| Category | Purpose | Example Parameters |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthropometric Data** | Define dummy size, weight, and segment inertia | Stature **1.75 m**, mass **78 kg**, seated height **0.91 m** |
| **Biomechanical Tolerance Data** | Define human injury thresholds | HIC limit **700**, Femur compression **10 kN**, Chest deflection **50 mm** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are testing a new helmet for a cricket batsman. You cannot repeatedly smash real players' heads with a cricket ball to see if the helmet is safe. Instead, you build a **head-shaped metal instrumented skull** — fitted with sensors that mimic the human brain's tolerance — and fire the ball at it. The sensors tell you how much "brain damage" the dummy would have suffered.

> [!TIP]
> The **Crash Test Dummy is to a real human exactly what the instrumented metal skull is to a cricketer.** It is a calibrated, repeatable, measurable substitute for a person who cannot ethically or practically be crashed.

Three "qualities" make an ATD indispensable:
1. **Biofidelity** — it must respond like a real human (joint stiffness, rib curvature, flesh compliance).
2. **Repeatability** — the same crash must give the same numbers within a tight statistical band.
3. **Durability** — it must survive hundreds of crash events without calibration drift.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Dummies Used Globally

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember at minimum the **Hybrid III 50th Male**, the **Hybrid III 5th Female**, and the **THOR** dummy — these are the workhorses of Euro NCAP, NHTSA, and IIHS protocols.

| Dummy | Population Represented | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Hybrid III 50th Male** | Average adult male (~1.77 m, 78 kg) | Frontal, side, rear impact tests |
| **Hybrid III 5th Female** | Small adult female (~1.50 m, 49 kg) | Low-mass occupant evaluation |
| **THOR (Test device for Human Occupant Restraint)** | Modern mid-size male | Advanced frontal & oblique testing |
| **WorldSID / ES-2** | Average male side impact | Side crash bio-fidelity |
| **SID-IIs** | Small female side impact | Side crash with small occupant |
| **Hybrid III 6YO / Q6 / Q10** | Child occupants | Child restraint systems |
| **CRABI 12 MO** | 12-month-old infant | Rearward-facing child seats |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **AIS – Abbreviated Injury Scale:** A medical scoring system from **0 (no injury)** to **6 (maximal, untreatable)** that anchors every numerical injury criterion (HIC, NIC, Femur Load) to a real clinical injury probability.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain: From Crash Pulse → Injury Risk

The entire discipline of crash ergonomics follows a deterministic five-step chain. Understanding *why* each step exists is the difference between rote learning and engineering mastery.

**Step 1 — Vehicle Crash Pulse Generation**
When two vehicles (or a vehicle and a rigid barrier) collide, the deceleration profile $a_v(t)$ of the occupant compartment is recorded. This pulse depends on the **crush zone stiffness**, **mass ratio**, and **impact velocity**.

**Step 2 — Dummy Kinematic Response**
The ATD, restrained by the seatbelt and airbag, experiences inertial loads. Internal sensors (accelerometers, load cells, potentiometers) record the *response* of each body segment.

**Step 3 — Region-Specific Injury Metric Calculation**
Raw sensor signals are mathematically processed into standardized injury criteria — HIC for the head, NIC for the neck, Chest Deflection for the thorax, Femur Load for the lower extremities.

**Step 4 — Comparison with Tolerance Limits**
Each calculated metric is compared to a threshold corresponding to a target **AIS level** (typically AIS 2+ probability of 50 % or AIS 3+ probability of 25 %).

**Step 5 — Safety Rating & Design Iteration**
A safety rating (Euro NCAP star, IIHS Top Safety Pick) is assigned, and the design team iterates the restraint geometry, airbag tuning, or seat stiffness.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-ready**. Memorize the threshold values — they are asked verbatim in the KTU university exams.

| # | Injury Criterion | Formula | Limit (Regulatory) | Body Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** | $HIC = \max\limits_{t_1, t_2} \left[ \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | $\le 700$ (Euro NCAP, $t_2-t_1 \le 15$ ms) / $\le 1000$ (FMVSS 208, $36$ ms) | Brain / Skull |
| 2 | **Resultant Head Acceleration** | $a_{res} = \sqrt{a_x^{\,2} + a_y^{\,2} + a_z^{\,2}}$ | $\le 80\,g$ peak (3 ms clip) | Brain |
| 3 | **Neck Injury Criterion (NIC)** | $NIC = 0.2\, a_{rel}(t) + v_{rel}(t)$ | $\le 15\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ | Cervical spine |
| 4 | **Neck Tension / Shear (Nij)** | $N_{ij} = \dfrac{F_{tension}}{F_{t,\,crit}} + \dfrac{M_{flexion}}{M_{y,\,crit}}$ | $\le 1.0$ | Neck |
| 5 | **Chest Compression** | $\delta_{chest}$ (mm) — measured by IRTRACC potentiometer | $\le 50$ mm (Euro NCAP) / $\le 63$ mm (FMVSS) | Thorax / Sternum |
| 6 | **Viscous Criterion (V*C)** | $V \cdot C = v_{sternum} \times \dfrac{d\delta_{chest}}{dt}$ | $\le 1.0\,\text{m/s}$ | Soft tissue thorax |
| 7 | **Femur Compression Load** | $F_{femur}$ | $\le 10\,\text{kN}$ (Euro NCAP) / $\le 8\,\text{kN}$ (FMVSS) | Lower limb |
| 8 | **Tibia Index (TI)** | $TI = \dfrac{M_{y,\,tibia}}{(M_{y})_{crit}} + \dfrac{F_{z,\,tibia}}{(F_z)_{crit}}$ | $\le 1.0$ | Lower leg |
| 9 | **Spine Acceleration** | $a_{T1}$ (T1 vertebra accelerometer) | $\le 60\,g$ (3 ms) | Lumbar spine |
| 10 | **3-ms Clip Acceleration** | Sustained $a \ge$ threshold for $\ge 3$ ms | Head $\le 80\,g$; Chest $\le 60\,g$ | All |

> [!NOTE]
> **Subscripts used in formulas:** $a_{rel}$ = relative acceleration between head CG and T1; $v_{rel}$ = relative velocity; $F_{crit}$, $M_{crit}$ = critical force/moment intercepts from PMHS (Post-Mortem Human Subject) tests.

## 2.3 Why Each Formula Exists — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| **HIC** | Sets the airbag deployment timing and airbag cushion stiffness — too high HIC means the airbag deployed too late or too softly. |
| **NIC** | Dictates seatbelt pretensioner fire-time; prevents "submarining" and excessive neck flexion under belt load. |
| **Chest Compression** | Drives steering wheel energy absorption, knee bolster stiffness, and seat back recline angle. |
| **Femur Load** | Determines knee bolster and lower IP panel stiffness to prevent dashboard-induced femur fractures. |
| **V*C** | A rate-sensitive criterion capturing the true energy absorbed by soft tissue (peak deflection alone misses velocity effect). |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's pearl:** HIC uses an exponent of **2.5** — not 2, not 3. This exponent comes from the Gadd Severity Index's empirical fit to skull fracture data. Marks are routinely lost for writing "HIC = a²".

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the HIC Formula

The Head Injury Criterion is **not** derived from a closed-form physics law — it is an empirical **dimensional reduction** of skull-fracture data. However, the KTU examiner expects a clean derivation starting from the **Gadd Severity Index (GSI)**.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Gadd Severity Index

Wayne Gadd (1966) experimentally impacted cadaver skulls and observed that concussive injury correlated with a weighted integral of acceleration raised to a power. He proposed:

$$
GSI = \int_0^T \left[a(t)\right]^n \, dt
$$

where the exponent $n$ is empirically fitted. Gadd found $n = 2.5$ provided the best linear separation between "injured" and "uninjured" cadaver test outcomes.

### 3.1.2 Refinement by Versace (1971)

Versace noted that GSI depended on the arbitrary total time $T$. He normalised the integral by the time interval $(t_2 - t_1)$ and raised the entire bracket to the power 2.5, separating the normalization factor outside:

$$
HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right\}
$$

Let us expand this step by step.

**Step (a) — Define the average resultant head acceleration over the interval $[t_1, t_2]$:**

$$
\bar{a}(t_1, t_2) = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt
$$

**Step (b) — Raise the average to the power 2.5 (the Gadd exponent):**

$$
\text{Peak Term} = \bar{a}(t_1, t_2)^{2.5}
$$

**Step (c) — Multiply by the time interval (Versace normalization):**

$$
HIC(t_1, t_2) = \left[ \bar{a}(t_1, t_2) \right]^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)
$$

**Step (d) — Take the maximum over all admissible intervals:**

$$
HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right\}
$$

**Step (e) — For HIC15, restrict $(t_2 - t_1) \le 15$ ms; for HIC36, restrict $\le 36$ ms.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Meaning:** HIC rewards *short, sharp* pulses less than *long, sustained* accelerations, because a 10 g pulse sustained for 50 ms is more injurious than a 30 g spike lasting 2 ms. The 2.5 exponent amplifies the average, while the $(t_2 - t_1)$ factor scales linearly with duration.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style)

A frontal crash test yields the following head-CG acceleration record (resultant magnitude):

| Time $t$ (ms) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a(t)$ (g) | 0 | 12 | 28 | 42 | 36 | 22 | 8 |

Compute HIC using the trapezoidal rule for $t_1 = 5$ ms, $t_2 = 25$ ms (a 20 ms interval — exceeds HIC15, so use HIC36 framework).

**Step 1 — Trapezoidal integration over $[5, 25]$ ms:**

$$
\int_{5}^{25} a(t)\, dt = \frac{5}{2}\bigl[(12+28) + (28+42) + (42+36) + (36+22)\bigr]
$$

$$
= 2.5 \times \bigl[40 + 70 + 78 + 58\bigr] = 2.5 \times 246 = 615 \quad \text{g·ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Average acceleration:**

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{615\,\text{g·ms}}{20\,\text{ms}} = 30.75\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Raise to power 2.5:**

$$
30.75^{2.5} = 30.75^{2} \times 30.75^{0.5} = 945.56 \times 5.545 \approx 5243.3
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by interval duration:**

$$
HIC_{20} = 5243.3 \times 20 = 104866
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with limit:**
$HIC \approx 1.05 \times 10^5 \gg 1000$ (FMVSS limit) — **test fails**, redesign required (likely airbag cushion stiffness too low or restraint timing too late).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Where Each Sensor Lives on a Hybrid III

| Anatomical Region | Dummy Sensor | Signal Output | Injury Criterion Fed |
|---|---|---|---|
| Head CG | Tri-axial accelerometer (3-axis) | $a_x, a_y, a_z$ | HIC, resultant $a$ |
| Upper Neck | 6-axis load cell (Fz, Fx, My) | Force & moment | $N_{ij}$, NIC |
| Chest (sternum) | IRTRACC potentiometer | $\delta_{chest}$ | Chest compression, V*C |
| Spine (T1, T12) | Uni-axial accelerometer | $a_{spine}$ | Spine acceleration |
| Lumbar spine | Load cell | Fz | Lower spine load |
| Femur (each) | Load cell | $F_{femur}$ | Femur compression |
| Tibia (upper, lower) | Load cell (Fx, My) | Force / moment | Tibia Index TI |
| Foot | Accelerometer | $a_{foot}$ | Foot/ankle injury |

> [!TIP]
> **Why so many sensors?** No single global acceleration captures injury risk. A 25 g whole-body acceleration may be harmless; the same 25 g *localised at the head* can cause skull fracture. The dummy's strength is **regional, not global, injury prediction**.

## 3.3 AIS — The Injury Probability Anchor

The **Abbreviated Injury Scale (AIS)** maps every numerical criterion to a clinical outcome probability:

| AIS | Severity | Example | Approximate % Survival |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | None | — | 100 % |
| 1 | Minor | Scalp laceration | 100 % |
| 2 | Moderate | Concussion with brief LOC | 99 % |
| 3 | Serious | Subdural haematoma | 95 % |
| 4 | Severe | Open skull fracture | 80 % |
| 5 | Critical | Brainstem contusion | 50 % |
| 6 | Maximal | Decapitation | 0 % |

Euro NCAP explicitly designs restraint systems to keep occupant injury risk **below 50 % probability of AIS 2+** for every body region.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ATD Instrumentation Chain

The figure below maps the data flow from physical crash event to final safety rating, illustrating how ergonomic data is transformed into engineering decisions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Impact Event Crash Pulse av t"] --> B["Anthropomorphic Test Device ATD Mounted on Sled or in Vehicle"]
    B --> C1["Head Tri axial Accelerometer 3 axis"]
    B --> C2["Neck 6 axis Load Cell Force Moment"]
    B --> C3["Chest IRTRACC Potentiometer Deflection"]
    B --> C4["Femur Load Cell Compression kN"]
    B --> C5["Tibia Load Cell Force Bending"]
    B --> C6["Spine T1 T12 Accelerometer"]
    C1 --> D["Data Acquisition System DAS 10 kHz Sampling"]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E["Signal Filtering CFC 60 SAE J211"]
    E --> F["Injury Criterion Calculator HIC NIC V C Femur"]
    F --> G["Threshold Comparison AIS 2 Plus Probability"]
    G --> H["Safety Rating Euro NCAP IIHS NHTSA Star or Top Safety Pick"]
    H --> I["Design Iteration Airbag Tuning Belt Pretensioner Seat Stiffness"]
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Biofidelity Hierarchy of Dummies (Visual Block Map)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Mechanical Dummy 1949 Sierra Sam"] --> B["Hybrid I 1971"]
    B --> C["Hybrid II 1976"]
    C --> D["Hybrid III 1976 to Present Global Workhorse"]
    D --> E["THOR 2009 Advanced Frontal"]
    D --> F["WorldSID 2005 Side Impact"]
    F --> G["SID IIs Small Female Side"]
    E --> H["Future THOR 50F Female 2025 Roadmap"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Hybrid III is a **mid-1970s design** that is still in service because its biofidelity for frontal impact is excellent and its calibration database spans 50+ years of cadaver correlation. Newer dummies (THOR, WorldSID) extend biofidelity to oblique and far-side impacts but are more expensive and harder to instrument.

## 4.3 Ergonomic Data Flow from Cadaver Test → Dummy → Vehicle

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["PMHS Cadaver Drop Tower Tests"] --> B["Biomechanical Tolerance Database Force vs Deflection"]
    B --> C["Dummy Component Calibration Rib Stiffness Neck Bending"]
    C --> D["Instrumented ATD Response Numbers"]
    D --> E["Injury Risk Function AIS Probability"]
    E --> F["Vehicle Restraint System Design"]
    F --> G["Final Vehicle Crash Test Verification"]
    G --> H["Public Star Rating"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term "Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)" and list any four commonly used ATDs in modern automotive crash testing.**

**Model Answer:**
An **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** is an instrumented mechanical surrogate of the human body used to measure biomechanical loads experienced by an occupant during a vehicle crash. It is constructed with segments (head, neck, thorax, pelvis, limbs) whose mass distribution, joint stiffness, and external geometry replicate the corresponding human anatomy.

Four commonly used ATDs are:

1. **Hybrid III 50th Male** — average adult male, frontal impact workhorse.
2. **Hybrid III 5th Female** — small adult female, low-mass occupant study.
3. **THOR** — advanced frontal impact with improved rib and shoulder biofidelity.
4. **WorldSID** — side impact dummy with realistic shoulder, thorax, and pelvis response.

*(Acceptable alternatives: ES-2, SID-IIs, Q6, Hybrid III 6YO, CRABI.)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)? State its regulatory limit as per Euro NCAP.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** is an empirical injury metric used to assess the risk of skull fracture and traumatic brain injury based on the resultant acceleration time-history of the head's centre of gravity.

It is computed as:

$$
HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right\}
$$

The **Euro NCAP limit** is **HIC15 ≤ 700** for an adult occupant (where the maximum time interval $(t_2 - t_1)$ is restricted to **15 ms**). A higher HIC corresponds to a higher probability of severe or fatal head injury.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the anatomy and instrumentation of a Hybrid III 50th Male dummy. Discuss the injury criteria evaluated at the head, neck, chest, and femur regions.**

#### **Part (a) — Dummy Anatomy & Instrumentation (7 Marks)**

The Hybrid III 50th Male dummy represents an average adult male of stature **1.77 m** and mass **78 kg**. Its principal components are:

| Region | Construction | Instrumentation |
|---|---|---|
| **Head** | Aluminium skull with vinyl "flesh" skin | Tri-axial accelerometer at CG (records $a_x, a_y, a_z$) |
| **Neck** | Segmented rubber/aluminium discs with cable + rubber stopper | 6-axis upper neck load cell (Fx, Fz, My) |
| **Thorax** | Six steel ribs with damping material | IRTRACC chest deflection potentiometer + sternum accelerometer |
| **Lumbar spine** | Steel cable with rubber discs | Uni-axial T12 accelerometer |
| **Pelvis** | Aluminium casting with vinyl skin | Pelvis accelerometer + iliac load cell |
| **Femur** | Steel tube with knee slider | Femur axial load cell |
| **Tibia** | Steel tube with rubber ankle | Upper/lower tibia load cell |
| **Feet** | Steel with rubber boot | Foot accelerometer + ankle load cell |
| **Joints** | Adjustable ball-and-socket (shoulder, elbow, hip) | — |
| **Skin** | Vinyl over foam for friction adjustment | — |

The dummy is calibrated per **SAE J2181** and **ISO 6487** channel frequency class **CFC 60** (1000 Hz filter).

**Model sketch (text description for exam):**
*(Draw a frontal-view dummy labelling: Head, Neck, Shoulder, Thorax, Lumbar, Pelvis, Femur, Knee, Tibia, Ankle, Foot. Mark the sensor locations with arrows: ① Head accelerometer, ② Neck load cell, ③ Chest IRTRACC, ④ T12 accelerometer, ⑤ Femur load cell, ⑥ Tibia load cell.)*

#### **Part (b) — Injury Criteria at Each Region (7 Marks)**

| Region | Criterion | Formula | Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Head** | HIC (15 ms) | $\left[\dfrac{1}{15}\int_{t}^{t+15} a\,dt\right]^{2.5}\cdot 15$ | $\le 700$ |
| **Head** | Resultant 3-ms accel | $\sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2}$ | $\le 80\,g$ |
| **Neck** | $N_{ij}$ | $\dfrac{F_z}{F_{z,\,crit}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{y,\,crit}}$ | $\le 1.0$ |
| **Neck** | NIC | $0.2\,a_{rel} + v_{rel}$ | $\le 15\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$ |
| **Chest** | Compression | $\delta_{chest}$ (mm) | $\le 50$ mm |
| **Chest** | Viscous V*C | $v_{sternum}\cdot d\delta/dt$ | $\le 1.0$ m/s |
| **Femur** | Compression load | $F_{femur}$ | $\le 10$ kN |

The dummy is instrumented such that each of these criteria is computed from the corresponding sensor data, post-processed via SAE J211 filtering, and compared with regulatory limits to assign an injury probability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a) Explain the Head Injury Criterion (HIC) in detail. Derive the expression for HIC and discuss its physical significance. (b) A frontal crash test gives the following head resultant acceleration time-history. Calculate the HIC for the interval $t_1 = 10$ ms to $t_2 = 25$ ms.**

| Time (ms) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $a$ (g) | 0 | 8 | 20 | 35 | 30 | 18 | 5 |

#### **Part (a) — HIC Theory (7 Marks)**

The Head Injury Criterion was developed by **John Versace in 1971** as a refinement of the **Gadd Severity Index (1966)**. It quantifies the likelihood of skull fracture and brain injury based on the resultant translational acceleration of the head's centre of gravity.

**Derivation (key steps):**

Gadd originally proposed:

$$
GSI = \int_0^T [a(t)]^n \, dt, \quad n = 2.5
$$

Versace normalised by the interval length $(t_2 - t_1)$ and took the maximum:

$$
HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1) \right\}
$$

**Physical significance:**
- The exponent **2.5** weights high accelerations heavily — small increases in peak $a$ dramatically raise HIC.
- The factor $(t_2 - t_1)$ ensures that **sustained** acceleration is more injurious than brief spikes.
- HIC15 uses a maximum interval of **15 ms** (Euro NCAP) and HIC36 uses **36 ms** (FMVSS 208).
- HIC $\le 700$ corresponds to low risk of AIS 3+ head injury; HIC $\ge 1000$ indicates high fatality risk.

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

Interval: $t_1 = 10$ ms, $t_2 = 25$ ms $\Rightarrow \Delta t = 15$ ms.

**Step 1 — Trapezoidal integration over $[10, 25]$ ms:**

$$
\int_{10}^{25} a(t)\, dt = \frac{5}{2}\bigl[(20+35) + (35+30) + (30+18)\bigr]
$$

$$
= 2.5 \times \bigl[55 + 65 + 48\bigr] = 2.5 \times 168 = 420 \;\text{g·ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Average acceleration:**

$$
\bar{a} = \frac{420}{15} = 28\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Raise to power 2.5:**

$$
28^{2.5} = 28^2 \cdot \sqrt{28} = 784 \times 5.2915 \approx 4148.5
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by interval duration (15 ms):**

$$
HIC_{15} = 4148.5 \times 15 = 62{,}228
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison:**
$HIC_{15} \approx 6.22 \times 10^4 \gg 700$ (Euro NCAP) — **test fails catastrophically**. The restraint system must be redesigned: likely actions include earlier airbag deployment, increased airbag venting, or stiffer knee bolster.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students routinely forget to convert the integration step (5 ms here) into the trapezoidal weighting factor. Marks are also lost for writing **$HIC = a^{2.5}$** without the time-window normalization. Always carry both the average-bracket and the $(t_2 - t_1)$ multiplier explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crash Test Dummies & Ergonomic Data**

- ☐ **ATD** = instrumented human surrogate; constructed from aluminium, vinyl, steel, rubber.
- ☐ The **Hybrid III 50th Male** is the global workhorse dummy (1.77 m, 78 kg).
- ☐ Dummy biofidelity must replicate **mass distribution, joint stiffness, geometry**.
- ☐ Every signal is filtered at **SAE J211 CFC 60** (1000 Hz).
- ☐ **HIC** uses exponent **2.5** — non-negotiable; **HIC15 ≤ 700** (Euro NCAP).
- ☐ **HIC36 ≤ 1000** (FMVSS 208) is the US federal limit.
- ☐ **NIC** = $0.2\,a_{rel} + v_{rel}$; threshold $\le 15\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$.
- ☐ **$N_{ij}$** combines axial neck force and bending moment; threshold $\le 1.0$.
- ☐ **Chest compression** measured by IRTRACC potentiometer; $\le 50$ mm (Euro NCAP).
- ☐ **V*C** captures rate-dependent soft-tissue injury; $\le 1.0$ m/s.
- ☐ **Femur load** $\le 10$ kN (Euro NCAP), $\le 8$ kN (FMVSS).
- ☐ **Tibia Index** $TI = M_y / M_{y,\,crit} + F_z / F_{z,\,crit} \le 1.0$.
- ☐ **AIS** maps every numerical criterion to clinical injury: **0 = none**, **6 = maximal**.
- ☐ Euro NCAP targets **≤ 50 % probability of AIS 2+** injury.
- ☐ New-generation dummies: **THOR** (frontal), **WorldSID** (side), **SID-IIs** (small female side).
- ☐ Calibration standards: **SAE J2181**, **ISO 6487**, **SAE J211**.
- ☐ Ergonomic data is the bridge between **cadaver tests → dummy → vehicle → star rating**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**H**ead-**H**IC, **N**eck-**N**IC-$N_{ij}$, **C**hest-**C**ompression-**V\*C**, **F**emur-**F**orce"* — remember **HNCF** in that order, and you will not miss any injury criterion in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle Interior Design for Impact Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT722 - AUTOMOTIVE ERGONOMICS AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle Interior Design for Impact Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Vehicle Interior Design for Impact Protection** is the systematic engineering discipline focused on configuring the passenger compartment (occupant space) of a motor vehicle to minimize injury severity to occupants during crash events. It encompasses the design of structural and non-structural interior elements—including the steering system, instrument panel, seats, head restraints, roof structure, doors, glazing, and supplementary restraint systems (SRS)—to manage kinetic energy dissipation, prevent occupant ejection, limit contact forces on human biodynamic tolerance thresholds, and maintain the integrity of the survival space throughout the crash pulse.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic is evaluated under **Course Outcome CO4**: *"Design and evaluate vehicle interior systems for occupant safety and impact protection."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Interior impact protection is governed by the biomechanical principle that occupant injury is a function of the **force, acceleration, displacement, and contact duration** transmitted to the human body during a crash. The interior must be designed to *lengthen the time over which the occupant decelerates*, thereby reducing peak loads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a raw egg being thrown at a concrete wall versus being thrown into a thick foam mattress:
- **Concrete Wall:** The egg stops almost instantly → very high deceleration → egg shatters.
- **Foam Mattress:** The egg travels a short distance over a longer time as the foam compresses → lower deceleration → egg survives.

**The vehicle interior is the "foam mattress" of the crash.** A rigid, hard interior would transfer the entire crash energy to the occupant in milliseconds. A well-designed interior **"cushions" the occupant by controlled deformation**, absorbing energy gradually.

| Crash Event Element | Real-World Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Vehicle front crumple zone | The "bumpers" of the mattress compressing first |
| Seat belt pretensioner | A hand that grabs the egg to slow it down |
| Airbag | An extra pillow inflating just before the egg arrives |
| Knee bolster & padded dash | The mattress filling the gap so the egg doesn't hit the hard frame |
| Collapsible steering column | A steering wheel that "gives way" rather than impaling the driver |

## 1.3 Key Metrics and Standard References

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Impact Protection Design (Bold for emphasis):**
> - **HIC (Head Injury Criterion):** Threshold value **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard FMVSS 208)
> - **Neck Injury Criterion (Nij):** Threshold **Nij ≤ 1.0**
> - **Chest Acceleration:** Limit **≤ 60 g** (3 ms exceedance, FMVSS 208)
> - **Femur Load:** Limit **≤ 10 kN** axial compression
> - **Survival Space:** Minimum **~925 mm × 1250 mm** elliptical envelope per ECE R94
> - **Crash Pulse Duration Target:** **~100–120 ms** for full frontal barrier impact

The core design objective is summarized by the **Kinematic Energy Management Equation:**

$$E_{k,\text{vehicle}} = \int F(t) \cdot v(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{vehicle}} v_{0}^{2}$$

The interior design must ensure that, of the total vehicle kinetic energy $E_k$, the portion transferred to occupants remains below human tolerance limits by extending the deceleration time $t$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Crash Energy Management Philosophy

The vehicle interior designer does **not** design to make the car "strong." The designer makes the car *selectively weak in the right places and strong in others*. This is the **"crush-up / survival space"** paradigm.

The kinetic energy of a moving vehicle is given by:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$$

For a typical 1500 kg car at 56 km/h (≈15.56 m/s) frontal barrier crash:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (15.56)^{2} = 181{,}600 \text{ J} \approx 181.6 \text{ kJ}$$

This is the energy that must be absorbed by the front structure, restrained occupants, and interior components without exceeding human tolerance.

## 2.2 Force–Time and Force–Displacement Analysis

The average occupant deceleration during a crash is governed by:

$$a_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$

Where:
- $\Delta v$ = change in velocity of occupant (≈ initial crash velocity for unrestrained, less for restrained)
- $\Delta t$ = stopping time (crash pulse duration)

**Engineering Implication:** To reduce $a_{\text{avg}}$, we must **increase $\Delta t$** by:
1. Increasing the **crush distance** $s$ of the front structure (crumple zone)
2. Lengthening the **restraint engagement time** via seatbelts, airbags, knee bolsters

The energy absorbed in a constant-force crush is:

$$E_{\text{absorbed}} = F_{\text{avg}} \cdot s$$

For an occupant restrained by a 3-point seatbelt with pretensioner and load limiter, the chest deceleration limit is kept below **60 g** (≈ 588 m/s²).

## 2.3 HIC (Head Injury Criterion) — Detailed

HIC is the most important single number in occupant impact protection. The HIC₁₅ formulation is:

$$\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \left( \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$$

Where:
- $a(t)$ = resultant head center-of-gravity acceleration, in units of **g**
- $t_1, t_2$ = two time instants (in seconds) such that $t_2 - t_1 \leq 36 \text{ ms}$ for HIC₃₆, or $\leq 15 \text{ ms}$ for HIC₁₅
- The exponent **2.5** is an empirical constant derived from cadaver and volunteer testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIC = 1000 corresponds to a ~16% probability of severe (AIS 4+) head injury, and ~50% probability of fatality (Mertz-Weber curve).** Designers aim for HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000; modern restraint systems achieve HIC₁₅ in the 400–700 range in 56 km/h barrier tests.

## 2.4 Neck Injury Criterion (Nij)

$$\text{N}_{ij} = \frac{F_{z}}{F_{z,c}} + \frac{M_{y}}{M_{y,c}}$$

Where:
- $F_z$ = axial neck tension/compression force
- $M_y$ = occipital bending moment
- $F_{z,c}$ and $M_{y,c}$ = critical biomechanical limits (≈ 4170 N tension, 6800 N compression, 310 Nm flexion, 135 Nm extension)

When $N_{ij} \leq 1.0$, the neck is considered within injury tolerance.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Formula** | **Equation** | **Threshold / Unit** | **Standard** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Vehicle kinetic energy | $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Universal |
| Average occupant deceleration | $a_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | m/s² or g | Universal |
| Energy absorbed in crush | $E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot s$ | Joules | Universal |
| Head Injury Criterion (HIC₁₅) | $\text{HIC} = \left[ \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \left( \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a \, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$ | ≤ 1000 (dimensionless) | FMVSS 208 |
| Neck Injury Criterion | $N_{ij} = \frac{F_z}{F_{z,c}} + \frac{M_y}{M_{y,c}}$ | ≤ 1.0 (dimensionless) | FMVSS 208 |
| Chest acceleration (3 ms) | $a_{\text{chest}}$ | ≤ 60 g | FMVSS 208 |
| Femur axial load | $F_{\text{femur}}$ | ≤ 10 kN | FMVSS 208 |
| Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI) | $\text{TTI} = \frac{a_{x,\text{upper}} \cdot a_{y,\text{upper}}}{2} + \frac{a_{x,\text{lower}} \cdot a_{y,\text{lower}}}{2}$ | ≤ 85 g (far side) | FMVSS 214 |
| Survival space (ECE R94) | Elliptical envelope | 925 × 1250 mm | ECE R94 |
| Crush efficiency | $\eta = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed,actual}}}{E_{\text{absorbed,ideal}}}$ | 0–100 % | Internal |
| Force-deflection (linear crush) | $F = k \cdot x$ | k = stiffness (N/m) | Universal |
| Stroke utilization | $S = \frac{x_{\text{actual}}}{x_{\text{available}}}$ | ≤ 100 % | Internal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The HIC equation is the cornerstone of airbag deployment testing. A frontal airbag is "tuned" to deploy with enough velocity to begin absorbing occupant head momentum by ~10 ms post-impact, and to be fully inflated by ~30 ms, ensuring the head contacts the cushion before it bottoms out against the steering wheel or dashboard.

## 2.6 Interior Components Mapped to Injury Reduction

| Interior Component | Primary Injury Mitigated | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Crumple zone (front) | All occupant injuries (indirectly) | Extends crash pulse time Δt |
| Collapsible steering column | Head, chest, neck (driver) | Telescopes 60–100 mm on impact |
| Padded instrument panel | Head, knee (passenger) | 25–50 mm EPS/EPP foam over plastic shell |
| Knee bolster | Femur, knee, pelvis (both) | Deforms 30–60 mm, transfers load to belt |
| Airbag (frontal) | Head, chest, neck | Vented cushion absorbs head kinetic energy |
| Side airbag / curtain | Head, thorax (side impact) | Covers window opening, head/chest zone |
| Padded A-pillar | Head (out-of-position) | 12–25 mm foam padding |
| Laminated windshield | Head, face (ejection) | PVB interlayer holds glass fragments |
| Energy-absorbing door | Thorax, pelvis (side) | Intrusion resistance + interior padding |
| 3-point belt with pretensioner | All (whole body) | Removes slack, engages load limiter |
| Head restraint | Neck (whiplash) | Limit retraction ≤ 60 mm, height ≥ 750 mm (ECE R17) |
| Reinforced roof | Head, spine (rollover) | Quasi-static roof strength ≥ 3× curb weight (FMVSS 216) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Deceleration of a Restrained Occupant

**Problem Setup:** A 1500 kg vehicle travelling at 56 km/h impacts a rigid fixed barrier head-on. The front crumple zone crushes 500 mm during the event. The restrained occupant has a stopping distance of 200 mm (relative to the vehicle) after seatbelt engagement. Determine the average deceleration of the vehicle and the average deceleration of the occupant.

### Step 1 — Convert Velocity to SI Units

$$v_0 = 56 \text{ km/h} = \frac{56 \times 1000}{3600} = 15.56 \text{ m/s}$$

This is the initial velocity of the vehicle just before impact.

### Step 2 — Apply the Work–Energy Theorem to the Vehicle

The total kinetic energy of the vehicle is:

$$E_{k,\text{veh}} = \frac{1}{2} m_{\text{veh}} v_0^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (15.56)^{2}$$

$$E_{k,\text{veh}} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 242.11 = 181{,}580 \text{ J} \approx 181.6 \text{ kJ}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Vehicle's Average Deceleration

The vehicle decelerates from 15.56 m/s to 0 over a crush distance of $s_{\text{veh}} = 0.500$ m. Using the kinematic equation:

$$v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s$$

$$0 = (15.56)^{2} + 2 \times a_{\text{veh}} \times 0.500$$

$$a_{\text{veh}} = -\frac{(15.56)^{2}}{2 \times 0.500} = -\frac{242.11}{1.000} = -242.11 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

Convert to g-units:

$$a_{\text{veh}} = \frac{242.11}{9.81} = 24.68 \text{ g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The vehicle's body decelerates at an average of **~24.7 g** because the crumple zone does the work of absorbing kinetic energy over 500 mm. The occupant does *not* experience 24.7 g, because the restraint system and airbag provide an additional stopping distance and time.

### Step 4 — Compute the Stopping Time of the Vehicle

Using $v = u + a t$:

$$0 = 15.56 + (-242.11) \times t_{\text{veh}}$$

$$t_{\text{veh}} = \frac{15.56}{242.11} = 0.0643 \text{ s} = 64.3 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Occupant's Average Deceleration

The occupant's stopping distance relative to the vehicle interior is $s_{\text{occ}} = 0.200$ m, but the occupant is also carried along with the deforming vehicle structure. We approximate the occupant's velocity-time profile as starting at 15.56 m/s (just after seatbelt engagement) and ending at 0 over an effective occupant stopping time. The occupant is restrained by the seatbelt and airbag, which together produce an effective stopping distance relative to the vehicle of 200 mm. The total occupant stopping distance relative to the ground is the vehicle's crush distance plus the occupant's relative motion:

$$s_{\text{occ,total}} = 0.500 + 0.200 = 0.700 \text{ m}$$

Applying the kinematic relation to the occupant:

$$a_{\text{occ}} = -\frac{v_0^{2}}{2 s_{\text{occ,total}}} = -\frac{(15.56)^{2}}{2 \times 0.700}$$

$$a_{\text{occ}} = -\frac{242.11}{1.400} = -172.94 \text{ m/s}^{2}$$

$$a_{\text{occ}} = \frac{172.94}{9.81} = 17.63 \text{ g}$$

### Step 6 — Validate Against Injury Thresholds

The computed occupant deceleration of **~17.6 g** is well below the **60 g** chest injury threshold from FMVSS 208, validating the interior design's effectiveness. If the interior had no crumple zone ($s_{\text{veh}} = 0$) and the occupant had to absorb all energy in 200 mm:

$$a_{\text{occ, no crush}} = \frac{242.11}{2 \times 0.200} = 605.3 \text{ m/s}^{2} = 61.7 \text{ g}$$

This would exceed injury tolerance, demonstrating why crumple zones are essential.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HIC Calculation for an Airbag-Equipped Driver

**Problem Setup:** During a 56 km/h barrier crash, the driver's head center-of-gravity acceleration (in g) is recorded as a triangular pulse with peak $a_{\text{peak}} = 45 \text{ g}$ at $t_{\text{peak}} = 18 \text{ ms}$ and the pulse returns to 0 at $t_{\text{end}} = 80 \text{ ms}$. Estimate HIC₁₅ for a 15 ms sliding window centered around the peak.

### Step 1 — Define the Acceleration Function

We approximate the triangular pulse as:

$$a(t) = \begin{cases} \frac{45}{18} \, t & 0 \leq t \leq 18 \text{ ms} \\ 45 \cdot \frac{80 - t}{80 - 18} & 18 \leq t \leq 80 \text{ ms} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This gives a rising slope of 2.5 g/ms and a falling slope of $-0.726$ g/ms.

### Step 2 — Choose the HIC Window

To capture the highest sustained acceleration, we choose a 15 ms window centered on the peak:

$$t_1 = 18 - 7.5 = 10.5 \text{ ms}$$
$$t_2 = 18 + 7.5 = 25.5 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Average Acceleration Over the Window

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t) \, dt$$

Approximating the average as the trapezoidal mean of the window endpoints:

$$a(t_1) = a(10.5) = 2.5 \times 10.5 = 26.25 \text{ g}$$
$$a(t_2) = a(25.5) = 45 \times \frac{80 - 25.5}{62} = 45 \times 0.879 = 39.56 \text{ g}$$

$$\bar{a} \approx \frac{26.25 + 39.56}{2} = 32.91 \text{ g}$$

### Step 4 — Compute HIC

$$\text{HIC} = \left( t_2 - t_1 \right) \times \left( \bar{a} \right)^{2.5}$$

Converting 15 ms to seconds: $\Delta t = 0.015$ s.

$$\text{HIC} = 0.015 \times (32.91)^{2.5}$$

Compute $(32.91)^{2.5}$:

$$(32.91)^{2.5} = (32.91)^{2} \times (32.91)^{0.5} = 1083.07 \times 5.737 = 6214$$

$$\text{HIC} = 0.015 \times 6214 = 93.2$$

### Step 5 — Validate

$$\text{HIC}_{15} = 93.2 \ll 1000$$

The driver's head is well protected. **The modern airbag + belt system delivers a HIC₁₅ more than 10× below the injury threshold**, providing a large safety margin.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Crush Energy Distribution

The total crash energy of 181.6 kJ must be absorbed by four energy-absorbing regions:

| **Region** | **Distance (mm)** | **Force (kN)** | **Energy Absorbed (kJ)** | **% of Total** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Front bumper beam | 50 | 200 | 10.0 | 5.5 % |
| Front longitudinal rails | 400 | 350 | 140.0 | 77.1 % |
| Occupant restraint (belt + airbag) | 200 | 80 | 16.0 | 8.8 % |
| Knee bolster + footwell | 100 | 156 | 15.6 | 8.6 % |
| **Total** | **750** | — | **181.6** | **100 %** |

This matrix shows the dominant role of the front rail crumple structure in energy management.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
